claims.claim: Electron beam radiolysis of ethylene carbonate (EC) produces branched oligomers with masses up to 1 to 2 kDa, as demonstrated by ESI MS/MS and NMR spectroscopy.
claims.quote: Extensive polymerization of ethylene carbonate (EC) leading to the formation of oligomers with masses up to 1 to 2 kDa in electron beam radiolysis is demonstrated using electrospray ionization mass spectrometry and nuclear magnetic resonance.

claims.claim: When EC containing 1 M LiPF6 was irradiated to a lower dose of 0.85 MGy, polymerization proceeded further than in neat EC, producing mass spectra extending to 1000–2000 a.m.u.
claims.quote: When EC containing 1 M LiPF6 was irradiated, even to a lower dose (0.85 MGy), the polymerization proceeded further (Figure 14S in the Supporting Information), producing mass spectra extending over to 1000−2000 a.m.u.

claims.claim: The presence of LiPF6 facilitates polymerization of EC under radiolysis but has little effect on the resulting polymer structure.
claims.quote: It appears that the presence of LiPF6 facilitates polymerization but has little eﬀect on the resulting polymer structure.

claims.claim: The extent of polymerization increases with radiation dose; at high dose, the largest oligomers become insoluble in water but retain excellent solubility in EC and polar organic solvents.
claims.quote: The extent of polymerization increases with radiation dose; at high dose, the largest of these oligomers become insoluble in water. However, such macromolecules exhibit excellent solubility in EC and polar organic solvents.

claims.claim: The radiolytically generated polymer from EC has a different structure than the linear (EO)x(EC)y copolymer produced by anionic polymerization of intact EC molecules, exhibiting chain branching and pendant carbonate groups.
claims.quote: This polymer has a diﬀerent structure and morphology than the linear chain copolymer of ethylene oxide and EC that is generated in anionic polymerization of intact EC molecules. This radiolytically generated polymer exhibits chain branching and pendant carbonate groups, and it can form a 3D organic network that is additionally cross-linked through lithium ions.

claims.claim: The probability of thermal runaway in commercial lithium-ion cells of type 18650 increases with increasing number of charge/discharge cycles and increasing state of charge (SOC).
claims.quote: a probability of thermal runaway in commercial lithium-ion cells of the type 18650 grows with number increase of charge/discharge cycles and increase of cells state of charge (SOC)

claims.claim: With increasing number of charge/discharge cycles, the initiation temperature of exothermic reactions of thermal runaway decreases considerably and the released energy increases.
claims.quote: with the number growth of cells charge/discharge cycles, it is observed a considerable decline of an initiation temperature of exothermic reactions of thermal runaway and increase of released energy

claims.claim: The energy released by the first group of exothermic reactions increases with increasing number of charge/discharge cycles: 0 kJ at NC=0, 1.08 kJ at NC=15, 2.14 kJ at NC=30, and 3.29 kJ at NC=45.
claims.quote: Energy of exothermic reactions of ﬁrst groupa (kJ) 0 / 0 ... 15 / 1.08 ... 30 / 2.14 ... 45 / 3.29

claims.claim: The temperature range of the first group of exothermic reactions and the energy released do not depend on SOC, but depend heavily on the number of charge/discharge cycles.
claims.quote: Temperature ranges of the ﬁrst group of the exothermic reactions do not depend on SOC (Table I and Fig. 2). Hence, also values of the energy released by the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group do not depend on SOC. However, they depend heavily on a number of cell charge/discharge cycles

claims.claim: For cells with NC=45, the current interrupt device is activated at approximately 75–85°C regardless of SOC, whereas for cells with NC=0 it is activated at approximately 115–150°C depending on SOC, indicating greater gas release in aged cells during the first group of exothermic reactions.
claims.quote: For cells with NC = 0 (which have no exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group, Fig. 2), the current interrupt device is activated at temperature values approximately 115–150◦C depending on SOC, Fig. 3c. For cells with NC = 45 (at which the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group do take place, Fig. 2), the current interrupt device is activated approximately at the temperature 75–85◦C regardless of SOC

claims.claim: Gas released from cells during the first group of exothermic reactions consisted of more than 99% hydrogen, with other gases (CO2, CO, CH4, C2H4) accounting for less than 1%.
claims.quote: The conducted measuring showed that the obtained gas from a cells consisted of hydrogen (more than 99%), while the other gases (CO2, CO, CH4 and C2H4) accounted for less than 1%

claims.claim: The heat released calculated from the hydrogen recombination reaction (Eq. 3) matched the heat amount measured from the first group of exothermic reactions within experimental error, proving that the first group of exothermic reactions is the recombination of atomic hydrogen accumulated in anode graphite.
claims.quote: an amount of heat released by a cell due to the exothermic reaction of recombination of atomic hydrogen accumulated in anode graphite (Table II) is equal to a heat amount (in limits of experimental error) found by us based on thermal effects observed for the ﬁrst group of the exothermic reactions (Table I)

claims.claim: The recombination of atomic hydrogen (H + H → H2) is a powerful exothermic reaction with heat emission of 436 kJ/mole of hydrogen, which is more intensive than the heat of hydrogen burning in oxygen at 285.8 kJ/mole of hydrogen.
claims.quote: The reaction (Eq. 3) is a powerful exothermic reaction with heat emission 436 kJ/mole (hydrogen). This heat emission is more intensive than in the reaction of hydrogen burning in oxygen and is equal to 285.8 kJ/mole (hydrogen).

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the temperature of thermal runaway shifts to lower values, connected with decreasing cathode thermal stability and increasing oxygen release from the cathode.
claims.quote: with growth of SOC, the temperature of the thermal runaway shifts to the side of lower values. This is connected with the fact that with the SOC growth, a cathode thermal stability falls, while oxygen release from a cathode grows.

claims.claim: Exothermic reactions of electrolyte thermal degradation on an anode occur in the temperature range 120–270°C, whereas the first group of exothermic reactions in aged cells appears in the range 32–116°C, indicating the first group cannot be attributed to electrolyte thermal degradation.
claims.quote: in the paper,31 it was shown that the exothermic reactions of electrolyte thermal degradation on an anode emerge in the temperature range from 120 to 270◦C, while the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group (Fig. 2) appear in the temperature range from 32 to 116◦C, i.e. at much lower temperature range

claims.claim: LIBs are most susceptible to thermal runaway in an adiabatic environment, followed by quasi-adiabatic and air environments, regardless of the charging rate.
claims.quote: LIBs are most susceptible to thermal runaway in an adiabatic environment, followed by quasi-adiabatic and air environments, regardless of the charging rate.

claims.claim: The time required for thermal runaway to occur (tTR) is longest in the air environment, followed by the quasi-adiabatic environment and adiabatic environment, indicating that heat dissipation is a critical factor in determining the speed at which thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: the time required for thermal runaway to occur (tTR) is longest in the air environment, followed by the quasi-adiabatic environment and adiabatic environment. The results suggest that heat dissipation is a critical factor in determining the speed at which thermal runaway occurs

claims.claim: Under all charging rate conditions, the time interval t1 (between tSR and tTR) follows the sequence: air environment > quasi-adiabatic environment > adiabatic environment, indicating that poor heat dissipation accelerates side-reactions and generates heat quickly.
claims.quote: Under all charging rate conditions, the values of t1 follow this sequence: air environment > quasi-environment > adiabatic environment, indicating that poor heat dissipation can accelerate side-reactions and generate heat quickly.

claims.claim: At 0.5 C overcharge in air, thermal runaway occurs at 329% SOC with ETR of 16.9 Wh, whereas at 1 C and 2 C, thermal runaway occurs at approximately 208% and 219% SOC with ETR of approximately 9.4 and 9.6 Wh respectively.
claims.quote: For batteries subjected to overcharging at 1 C or 2 C, all the batteries experience thermal runaway at approximately 210% SOC and the charging energy at thermal runaway (ETR) is approximately 9.5 Wh. For a battery overcharged at 0.5 C, thermal runaway occurs at 329% SOC, and the ETR is 16.9 Wh.

claims.claim: When overcharged at 0.5 C in quasi-adiabatic environment, SOCTR is 200%, compared to 309% in air environment at the same rate, demonstrating that heat dissipation quality plays a crucial role in preventing thermal runaway particularly at low charging rates.
claims.quote: when overcharged at a rate of 0.5 C, the SOCTR of the battery is as high as 309% in the air environment, whereas it is only 200% in the quasi-adiabatic environment. Thus, the quality of heat dissipation plays a crucial role in preventing battery thermal runaway, particularly at low charging rates.

claims.claim: The RSEI increases sharply at 155% and 165% SOC, attributed not only to SEI thickening but also to lithium deposition at the anode, with RSEI values rising from 0.010074 Ω at 100% SOC to 1.762000 Ω at 165% SOC.
claims.quote: we suspect that the sharp rise in RSEI at 155% and 165% SOC is due not only to the thickening of the SEI but also to the deposition of lithium at the anode

claims.claim: The lithium ion diffusion coefficient decreases with increasing SOC, and overcharging significantly reduces lithium ion diffusion capacity due to increases in Rs, RSEI, Rct, electrolyte decomposition, and lithium deposition on the anode surface.
claims.quote: The results show that the diffusion coefficient decreases with increasing SOC, and overcharging significantly reduces the lithium ion diffusion capacity due to the increase in Rs, RSEI, Rct, as well as electrolyte decomposition and lithium deposition on the surface of anode.

claims.claim: Starting from 140% SOC, obvious gaps appear between cathode particles, and the gaps increase further at 155% and 165% SOC, indicating that the cathode structure becomes obviously loose under high voltage.
claims.quote: Starting from 140% SOC, there are obvious gaps between particles (marked by red circles), and there are more and more gaps at 155% and 165% SOC. This shows that under the high voltage environment, the cathode structure is obviously loose

claims.claim: At SOC ≥ 140%, obvious lithium deposition appears on the anode surface and traces of Mn, Co, and Ni transition metal elements are detected by EDS, attributed to dissolution of Ni, Co, and Mn compounds in the electrolyte and migration to the anode during prolonged over-delithiation.
claims.quote: SOC ≥ 140%, there is a significant change. From Fig. 12b-3, it can be seen that there is obvious lithium deposition on the anode surface of 140% SOC, and traces of Mn, Co and Ni transition metal elements appear in EDS, which is because Ni, Co, and Mn compounds dissolving in the electrolyte and reaching the anode during prolonged over-delithiation at high operating voltages

claims.claim: The duration from battery rupture to ignition did not exceed 10 seconds, indicating that battery rupture must be prevented during use to avoid thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the duration from rupture to ignition did not exceed 10 seconds, indicating that the battery should be prevented from rupturing during use to avoid thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The time interval between thermal runaway and Tmax (T2) was always below 50 seconds under all test conditions.
claims.quote: Our investigation revealed that the time interval between thermal runaway and Tmax (T2) was always below 50 s, providing a theoretical basis for extinguishing LIB fires.

claims.claim: In the adiabatic environment, only one voltage plateau is observed during overcharging at 2 C because the temperature range of Stage II (66.5–93.5 °C) causes both SEI decomposition and electrolyte reaction with embedded lithium to occur simultaneously.
claims.quote: The temperature range of Stage II is 66.5-93.5 ℃ due to the rapid temperature rise, and both the decomposition of the SEI film and the reaction of the electrolyte with embedded lithium occur during this stage, leading to the occurrence of only one voltage plateau.

claims.claim: At 0.5 C charging rate in air, a temperature buffering period exists with a temperature rise rate of only 0.003 °C/s, during which stopping overcharge can prevent thermal runaway; this buffering period is not observed at 1 C and 2 C.
claims.quote: At a charging rate of 0.5 C, the battery exhibits a significant temperature buffering period, during which the temperature rise rate is only 0.003 ℃/s. If overcharging is stopped during this stage, it can prevent thermal runaway. However, such a stage is not observed at charging rates of 1 C and 2 C.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway under adiabatic conditions is approximately 720 °C with little variation across charging rates, while Tmax shows large differences in air and quasi-adiabatic environments due to airflow effects.
claims.quote: Tmax of LIBs has little difference during thermal runaway under adiabatic environment (about 720 ℃), however, there are much big differences in the air and quasi-adiabatic environment. Tmax is mainly related to the heat generated during the combustion process after rupturing. Therefore, affected by the air flow, the difference of Tmax is large in non-adiabatic environments.

claims.claim: At 100% and 120% SOC, the cathode is the primary contributor to heat production (≥80%), but at ≥140% SOC, the anode becomes the dominant heat source (about 60%) due to heat generated by deposited lithium and electrolyte.
claims.quote: when the LIBs were at 100% and 120% SOC, the cathode was the primary contributor to heat production (≥ 80%). However, at ≥ 140% SOC, the anode became the dominant source of heat (about 60%), due to the significant amount of heat generated by the deposited lithium and electrolyte.

claims.claim: The initial reduction products of ethylene carbonate (EC) on the graphite anode are lithium ethylene dicarbonate (LEDC) and ethylene, with ethylene being the only gas evolved during initial formation cycles as observed by OEMS analysis of 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC.
claims.quote: OEMS analysis of 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC provides consistent results with the observation of ethylene as the only gas evolved during the initial formation cycles. Thus, the initial SEI formed in 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC is predominantly LEDC and LiF.

claims.claim: Storage of Li2CO3 in the presence of LiPF6 in DMC results in quantitative decomposition of Li2CO3 after only 2 days at 55°C, producing LiF, carbon dioxide, and lithium difluorophosphate.
claims.quote: Storage of Li2CO3 in the presence of LiPF6 in DMC results in the quantitative decomposition of Li2CO3 after only 2 days at 55°C. The products of this decomposition reaction are LiF, carbon dioxide, and lithium difluorophosphate (Figure 2B).

claims.claim: Upon storage of LEDC in the presence of LiPF6 in DMC, LEDC quantitatively decomposes at 55°C after 2 days, generating decomposition products including LiF, fluorophosphates, trimethyl phosphate, carbon dioxide, and oligoethylene oxides.
claims.quote: Upon storage of LEDC in the presence of LiPF6 in DMC, LEDC quantitatively decomposes at only 55°C after 2 days. However, the reaction mixture is much more complicated with LEDC than with Li2CO3. The decomposition products include LiF, fluorophosphates, trimethyl phosphate, carbon dioxide, and oligoethylene oxides.

claims.claim: Because many decomposition products of Li2CO3 or LEDC reacting with LiPF6 are either gases or electrolyte-soluble, the quantity of insoluble SEI components decreases and the SEI becomes more porous.
claims.quote: It is important to note that many of the decomposition products of Li2CO3 or LEDC with LiPF6 are either gases or are soluble in the electrolyte. This suggests that upon reaction of the lithium carbonates with LiPF6 the quantity of the insoluble SEI components are decreasing and the SEI is becoming more porous.

claims.claim: The reduction of VC inhibits the reduction of EC, resulting in a decrease in the generation of LEDC and ethylene, and VC is preferentially reduced to generate poly(VC) and CO2, with the CO2 further reduced to generate Li2CO3.
claims.quote: The reduction reactions of VC have been reported to inhibit the reduction of EC resulting in a decrease in the generation of LEDC and ethylene. VC is preferentially reduced to generate poly(VC) and CO2. As discussed above, the CO2 generated from the decomposition of VC is further reduced to generate Li2CO3.

claims.claim: The 100% SOC Li-S pouch cell presents an onset self-heating temperature (Tonset) of 82°C and a thermal runaway temperature (Ttr) of 181°C under ARC heat-wait-search testing.
claims.quote: the 100% SOC Li-S pouch cell presents a typical HWS curve, with a Tonset of 82°C and a Ttr of 181°C

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the Li-S pouch cell onset self-heating temperature increases to 97°C and thermal runaway temperature increases to 262°C, compared to 82°C and 181°C at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: after being fully discharged (0% SOC), the Tonset and Ttr of the pouch cell increase to 97°C and 262°C, respectively

claims.claim: Reduction of sulfur content in the cathode after discharging only slows down but cannot eliminate the disastrous thermal runaway of Li-S batteries.
claims.quote: the reduction of sulfur content in cathode after discharging only slows down, but cannot eliminate, the disastrous thermal runaway

claims.claim: A pristine Li-S pouch cell without electrolyte goes into severe thermal runaway at a low Ttr of 121°C, which is lower than the 0% SOC cell (262°C) and 100% SOC cell (181°C), indicating that removing flammable liquid electrolyte does not prevent thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the pristine Li-S pouch cell without electrolyte goes severe thermal runaway immediately at a low Ttr of 121°C

claims.claim: A pouch cell with low electrolyte-to-sulfur (E/S) ratio undergoes sudden thermal runaway at 176°C because lack of electrolyte accelerates direct contact of melting cathode active materials and metallic lithium in the anode.
claims.quote: pouch cell with low E/S ratio undergoes sudden thermal runaway at 176°C, indicating that the lack of electrolyte will accelerate the direct contact of melting cathode active materials and metallic lithium in anode, which consequently induces tremendous heat generation in a short time

claims.claim: The electrolyte/anode system reaches a maximum self-heating rate (dT/dtmax) of 287°C/min, compared to 207°C/min for electrolyte/cathode and 119°C/min for electrolyte alone, demonstrating that electrolyte–anode thermal compatibility most strongly accelerates self-heating.
claims.quote: the cycled electrolyte individually presents a relatively low SHR, with a dT/dtmax of 119°C/min, whereas those for Ele with Ca and Ele with An are 207 and 287°C/min, respectively

claims.claim: The presence of soluble polysulfide in cycled electrolyte decreases the onset self-heating temperature to 78°C but increases the thermal runaway temperature to 327°C compared to pristine electrolyte (Tonset 135°C, Ttr 298°C), indicating polysulfide triggers early self-heating but alleviates rapid thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the cycled Ele shows decreased thermal stability with a lower Tonset of 78°C and a higher Ttr of 327°C

claims.claim: The all-solid-state Li-S cell employing Li6PS5Cl solid electrolyte undergoes thermal runaway at 190°C despite the SSE/cathode system showing no remarkable self-heating up to 250°C, demonstrating that inorganic solid-state electrolyte cannot eliminate thermal runaway in large-format Li-S cells.
claims.quote: for the whole SSE cell test, self-heating is unexpectedly detected at a rather low temperature of 90°C, and then it keeps self-heating until thermal runaway at 190°C… even employing inorganic solid-state electrolyte cannot eliminate the thermal runaway behavior of the large-format Li-S pouch cells

claims.claim: The pressure at the thermal runaway point (Ptr) for electrolyte alone, electrolyte with cathode, and electrolyte with anode are 17, 15, and 9 bar respectively, demonstrating that electrolyte evaporation is the largest contributor to pressure buildup before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The Ptr of the Ele, Ele with Ca, and Ele with An are 17, 15, and 9 bar, respectively, demonstrating that the electrolyte evaporating is the largest contributor for increasing pressure before and during thermal runaway

claims.claim: Sulfur cathode sublimation causes the lithium metal anode to self-heat at 93°C under gaseous cross-talk conditions where solid electrodes remain uncontacted, confirming that sublimating sulfur vapor can trigger exothermic redox reactions at the anode.
claims.quote: the lithium metal anode presents self-heating at 93°C, indicating that the sublimating cathode sulfur reacts with the anode materials and results in exothermic redox reaction

claims.claim: Polycrystalline LiNi0.88Co0.07Mn0.05O2 exhibits higher decomposition temperature and lower decomposition rate than single-crystalline LiNi0.88Co0.07Mn0.05O2, contributing to better thermal stability.
claims.quote: polycrystalline LiNi0.88Co0.07Mn0.05O2 exhibits higher decomposition temperature and lower decomposition rate than that in single-crystalline counterparts, contributing to better thermal stability.

claims.claim: For delithiated SC-NCM, the mean activation energy values are 36.74, 180.19, and 160.64 kJ/mol for Step 1, Step 2, and Step 3, respectively, while for PC-NCM the mean activation energy values are 44.96, 197.93, and 291.11 kJ/mol for each step.
claims.quote: As for SC-NCM, the mean E values are 36.74, 180.19, and 160.64 kJ mol−1 for each step, whereas for PC-NCM, the mean E values are 44.96, 197.93, and 291.11 kJ mol−1, respectively.

claims.claim: The activation energy for each decomposition step of PC-NCM is larger than that of SC-NCM, indicating that the thermal stability of PC-NCM is better than SC-NCM.
claims.quote: the E for each step of PC-NCM is larger than that of SC-NCM, which means that the thermal stability of PC-NCM is better than SC-NCM.

claims.claim: SC-NCM exhibits a higher rate constant for the exothermal phase transition steps (Step 2 and Step 3) compared to PC-NCM, implying faster decomposition and less thermal stability for SC-NCM.
claims.quote: Step2 and Step3 are highly exothermal, while SC-NCM exhibits higher k (Fig. 7b and c), where faster decomposition against thermal stability is implied.

claims.claim: The average weight loss rate during heating is approximately 14.53% for SC-NCM and approximately 10.00% for PC-NCM, implying different thermal stability between the two morphologies.
claims.quote: Table S1 reveals that the average weight loss rate is ~ 14.53 % for SC-NCM and ~ 10.00 % for PC-NCM, which implies their different thermal stability during heating.

claims.claim: At 25% and 50% SOC, thermal runaway in SIBs is dominated by SEI decomposition and limited electrolyte breakdown, leading to minor gas release and non-explosion venting.
claims.quote: At low SOC (25 % and 50 %), the thermal response is primarily driven by SEI decomposition and limited electrolyte degradation, producing minor gas emissions insufficient to trigger mechanical failure, resulting in non-explosion white smoke venting.

claims.claim: At 75% SOC, SIBs exhibit a critical transition state where microstructural variability determines whether failure remains non-explosion or escalates to violent explosion, as evidenced by three explosions and four non-explosions in seven identical tests.
claims.quote: At intermediate SOC (75 %), the battery enters a critical transition state where more active electrode materials begin to participate in exothermic reactions. However, whether these reactions escalate into an explosion depends on microstructural variability and the dynamic balance between heat release and pressure relief. This was evidenced by the observation that among seven tests at 75 % SOC, three resulted in explosions while four did not, despite identical pre-screened battery parameters including capacity, voltage, and internal resistance.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, extensive decomposition of both electrolyte and electrode materials generates a large volume of flammable gases in a short time, overwhelming the battery's venting capacity and inevitably leading to a catastrophic explosion.
claims.quote: At high SOC (100 %), extensive decomposition of both electrolyte and electrode materials generates a large volume of flammable gases in a short time, overwhelming the battery's venting capacity and inevitably leading to a catastrophic explosion.

claims.claim: Peak pressure Pmax increases approximately 450 times from 25% SOC to 50% SOC, attributed to higher electrolyte concentration and increased stored energy intensifying chemical reactions and accelerating pressure buildup.
claims.quote: as SOC increases, both Tmax and Pmax rise noticeably, with Pmax increasing by approximately 450 times from 25 % SOC to 50 % SOC. This increase is attributed to higher electrolyte concentration and increased stored energy in the battery under high SOC conditions, intensifying chemical reactions and accelerating pressure buildup.

claims.claim: At 75% SOC, the maximum pressure rise rate (dP/dt)Max in the explosion case is approximately five times higher than in the non-explosion case, primarily due to shock wave generation during mechanical rupture, while maximum temperature rise rates remain comparable between the two cases.
claims.quote: Specifically, (dT/dt)Max remains comparable between the two cases, indicating similar surface heating dynamics. However, (dP/dt)Max in the explosion case is approximately five times higher than in the non-explosion case, primarily due to the generation of a shock wave during mechanical rupture.

claims.claim: The non-explosion battery at 75% SOC exhibits a higher maximum temperature (Tmax) than the exploded battery at the same SOC, because explosion scenarios are dominated by rapid gas expansion that overwhelms structural integrity before maximum thermal output is reached.
claims.quote: under the 75 % SOC condition, the non-explosion battery exhibits a higher Tmax than the explosion one, despite the latter experiencing catastrophic failure. This mismatch in the heat–pressure response suggests that explosion risk is not solely governed by the extent of heat accumulation but is more critically constrained by the system's ability to relieve pressure within a short time frame.

claims.claim: Compared to LIBs, SIBs exhibit higher first voltage drop temperatures and lower maximum TR temperatures, but SIBs with 75% and 100% SOC generate significantly higher explosion pressures in confined conditions.
claims.quote: Compared to LIBs, SIBs exhibit higher first voltage drop temperatures but lower maximum TR temperatures, reflecting the superior thermal stability of their sodium-based materials. However, SIBs with 75 % and 100 % SOC generate significantly higher explosion pressures, posing substantial mechanical and safety risks under confined conditions.

claims.claim: Normalized gas emission analysis reveals that batteries at 50% SOC present the highest cumulative gas release and heat release risk among non-exploding conditions, indicating a non-linear relationship between SOC and cumulative risk.
claims.quote: standardizing the total gas release and heat release of the batteries with different SOCs reveals that the battery with 50 % SOC has the greatest accumulative risk, indicating a complex non-linear relationship between cumulative risk and SOC.

claims.claim: Under explosion conditions, as SOC decreases from 100% to 75%, the percentage of combustible gases decreases while the percentage of CO2 increases, reflecting more complete combustion at higher SOC levels.
claims.quote: Under explosion conditions, as the SOC decreases, the percentage of combustible gases decreases while the percentage of CO2 increases, which may be related to the equivalent ratio of combustible gases to oxygen.

claims.claim: As SOC decreases from 75% to 25%, the proportion of hydrocarbon gases decreases from 4.7% to 2.3%, while the proportion of CO increases from 13.5% to 21%, indicating that low charge state promotes CO generation reactions in SIBs.
claims.quote: when the battery's state of charge decreased from 75 % to 25 %, the proportion of hydrocarbon gases decreased from 4.7 % to 2.3 %, while the proportion of CO increased from 13.5 % to 21 %, indicating that the low-charge state of the battery is more prone to CO generation reactions.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition begins at temperatures above 130°C.
claims.quote: Side reactions, including a breakdown of the Solid Electrolyte Interface (SEI) layer, will occur at elevated temperatures above 130◦C

claims.claim: Anode decomposition (intercalated lithium reacting with electrolyte) starts at around 180°C.
claims.quote: At higher temperature, around 180 ◦C, intercalated lithium in anode graphite starts to react with electrolyte.

claims.claim: For NMC battery chemistry, cathode decomposition side reaction starts at 240°C.
claims.quote: For the NMC battery chemistry, this side reaction usually starts at 240 ◦C

claims.claim: In a simulation of a single 18650 NMC cell undergoing thermal runaway via external short circuit, the maximum temperature reached over 800°C and 7.5 mmol CO2 was released as first venting gas.
claims.quote: the battery experienced a thermal runaway event and the maximum temperature reached over 800 ◦C. According to the simulation result, there will be 7.5 mmol CO2 as first venting gas.

claims.claim: In a 55-gallon drum simulation, gas sensing detected thermal runaway at 85 seconds, which is before the propagation critical time of 710 seconds, while drum surface temperature change was less than 0.001°C at 710 seconds and could not trigger detection before propagation.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway can be detected at 85 seconds, which is ahead of the thermal runaway critical time (tcrit = 710s)... at t = 710s, the surface temperature change is less than 0.001 ◦C and this small change can hardly be detected with a temperature sensor.

claims.claim: A broad-scale internal short circuit event can cause the cell temperature to rapidly rise to 300°C or higher, leading to cathode material decomposition and massive oxygen release.
claims.quote: This broad-scale ISC event can cause the cell temperature to rapidly rise to 300°C or higher, leading to cathode material decomposition and massive oxygen release.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway propagation at the module level depends on the single cell's heat dissipation and absorption capabilities.
claims.quote: TRP at the module level depends on the single cell's heat dissipation and absorption capabilities.

claims.claim: Heat release rates during fire propagation at the system level can rapidly escalate to the MW scale, potentially igniting other combustible components and leading to catastrophic fire incidents.
claims.quote: The heat release rates (HRRs) during fire propagation at the system level can rapidly escalate to the MW scale, potentially igniting other combustible components and leading to catastrophic fire incidents.

claims.claim: Thermodynamic parameters obtained from calorimetry tests are specific to one chemical system or state, as thermal runaway performance is influenced by materials, state of charge (SOC), and state of health (SOH).
claims.quote: thermodynamic parameters obtained from calorimetry tests are specific to one chemical system or state, as TR performance is influenced by materials, state of charge (SOC), and state of health (SOH).

claims.claim: Even if ISC is induced, the joule heat from ISC may be limited by sharply increased battery resistance, thus contributing minimally to thermal runaway heat generation.
claims.quote: even if ISC is induced, the joule heat from ISC may be limited by sharply increased battery resistance, thus contributing minimally to TR heat generation.

claims.claim: ARC tests of fresh and aged Li-ion cells at 70% state of health demonstrated an earlier onset of self-heating due to exothermic reactions between the plated Li and electrolyte.
claims.quote: Accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) tests of fresh and aged Li-ion cells (at 70 % state of health) demonstrated an earlier onset of self-heating due to exothermic reactions between the plated Li and electrolyte

claims.claim: The ratio of total heat released during thermal runaway to the electrochemical energy in the cell increases with cycle number, indicating a strong correlation between TR interactions and capacity degradation.
claims.quote: the ratio of the total heat released during TR to the electrochemical energy in the cell is reported to increase with cycle number, implying a strong correlation between the TR interactions and capacity degradation

claims.claim: At low temperatures, aging of Li-ion cells is dominated by Li plating, whereas at high temperatures, aging is dominated by SEI growth, electrolyte decomposition, and electrode active material losses.
claims.quote: At low temperatures, aging is dominated by Li plating, whereas, at high temperatures, aging is dominated by solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, electrolyte decomposition, and electrode active material losses

claims.claim: Li-ion cells exhibit a minimum degradation rate at 25°C, with an aggravated degradation rate when deviating from this temperature.
claims.quote: Li-ion cells exhibiting a minimum degradation rate at 25 ◦C and an aggravated rate when deviating from this temperature

claims.claim: Under cycling at 1.5C and 10°C, the dominant aging mechanism is lithium plating, followed by anode active material loss, cathode active material loss, and SEI growth on the anode.
claims.quote: under cycling at 1.5C, 10 ◦C, the dominant aging mechanism is lithium plating, followed by anode active material loss, cathode active material loss, and SEI growth on the anode

claims.claim: The characteristic temperature T1 for onset of abnormal heat generation in LIB thermal runaway initiation stage is usually at 60–120 °C.
claims.quote: This temperature increase drives the battery to the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation (characteristic temperature T1, usually at 60 ~ 120 °C).

claims.claim: The characteristic temperature T2 for triggering thermal runaway in the propagation stage is usually lower than 300 °C.
claims.quote: This propels the battery towards the triggering temperature of thermal runaway (characteristic temperature T2, usually lower than 300 °C).

claims.claim: Plated lithium on graphite anodes decreases the characteristic temperatures T1 and T2, significantly reducing the thermal stability of the LIB.
claims.quote: the plated lithium can decrease the characteristic temperatures T1 and T2 (as shown in Figure 2d) and significantly reduce the thermal stability of the LIB.

claims.claim: During lithiation of Li4Ti5O12, the unit cell volume expands by merely 0.2%, leading to it being referred to as a 'zero-strain' material.
claims.quote: the lithiation process from Li4Ti5O12 to Li7Ti5O12 increases the unit cell parameter from 8.3595 to 8.3598 Å, and the unit cell volume expands by merely 0.2%. This minimal change leads to Li4Ti5O12 often being referred to as a 'zero-strain' material.

claims.claim: The LTO anode slows the short-circuit reaction by three orders of magnitude compared to a graphite anode.
claims.quote: The LTO anode also contributed significantly to safety, slowing the short-circuit reaction by three orders of magnitude compared to a graphite anode.

claims.claim: After nail penetration of the 49 Ah TNO/NCM pouch cell, the voltage gradually decreased to 1.0 V over 100 minutes while the cell temperature increased to a maximum of only 48 °C.
claims.quote: After nail penetration, the voltage of their TNO/NCM pouch cell gradually decreased to 1.0 V over 100 minutes, while the cell temperature increased to a maximum of only 48 °C.

claims.claim: Nanosized Li4Ti5O12, due to its larger electrolyte/electrode surface area, encounters more extensive side reactions with the electrolyte, exacerbating the gas evolution problem.
claims.quote: Nanosized Li4Ti5O12, due to its larger electrolyte/electrode surface area, often encounters extensive side reactions with the electrolyte, thereby exacerbating the gas evolution problem.

claims.claim: The conductivity of LiTi2O4 is 5.56 × 10^6 S cm−1, which is far superior to the approximately 10^−9–10^−13 S cm−1 of Li4Ti5O12.
claims.quote: the conductivity of LiTi2O4 is 5.56 × 106 S cm−1, which is far superior to the approximately 10−9–10−13 S cm−1 of Li4Ti5O12

claims.claim: When TiNb2O7 is charged and discharged in the range of 1.0–2.5 V, it can deliver a reversible specific capacity of 285 mAh g−1, and the volume expansion is only 7.22%.
claims.quote: When charged and discharged in the range of 1.0–2.5 V, TiNb2O7 can deliver a reversible specific capacity of 285 mAh g−1. Lithium plating is less likely to occur in this voltage range, and the volume expansion is only 7.22%

claims.claim: Vacuum annealing of TiNb2O7 improved its electronic conductivity from 10^−11 to 10^−3 S cm−1 by introducing oxygen vacancies.
claims.quote: vacuum annealing of TiNb2O7 introduces oxygen vacancies and partially reduces Ti4+ to Ti3+ and Nb5+ to Nb4+. This process significantly improved the electronic conductivity of TiNb2O7 from 10−11 to 10−3 S cm−1

claims.claim: Batteries cycled at -5°C/1C exhibited rapid degradation and reached end of life after only 60 cycles, whereas batteries cycled at 25°C/2C and 55°C/1C reached cycle numbers of 2820 and 1020, respectively.
claims.quote: the batteries cycled at -5 °C exhibited rapid degradation and reached EoL after only 60 cycles, whereas the batteries cycled at higher temperatures exhibited better performance, with the cycle numbers reaching 2820 and 1020 at 25 °C and 55 °C, respectively.

claims.claim: For batteries cycled at -5°C/1C, T1 decreased from 72.39°C to 52.40°C when SOH dropped from 100% to 77.5%, and T2 dropped by 23°C (from 213.3°C to 190.2°C), while DtTR decreased by 70% when SOH reached 80%.
claims.quote: For the batteries cycled at -5 °C/1C, T1 decreased from 72.39 °C to 52.40 °C when the SOH dropping from 100% to 77.5%. T2 also dropped by 23 °C (from 213.3 °C to 190.2 °C) after cycling at -5 °C/1C, while T3 remained constant at different SOH. DtTR became much shorter as the batteries degraded and decreased by 70% when SOH reached 80%.

claims.claim: Batteries stored at 55°C/100% SOC showed improved thermal runaway performance, with T1 rising to 96.3°C and DtTR reaching 2.85 times its initial value.
claims.quote: The batteries stored at 55 °C/100%SOC showed improved TR performance, with T1 rising to 96.3 °C and DtTR reaching 2.85 times its initial value.

claims.claim: Lithium plating on the anode is the key reason for deterioration of battery thermal runaway performance during aging, leading to drastic increase in temperature rate and obvious decreases in T1, T2, and DtTR.
claims.quote: Lithium plating on the anode leads to severe deterioration of the battery TR performance, with drastic increase in the temperature rate and obvious decrease in T1, T2, and DtTR.

claims.claim: The thermal stability of degraded cathodes remained nearly the same as that of the fresh cathode under all four aging paths, while the evolution of battery thermal runaway behavior mainly depended on changes in the thermal stability of the anode+electrolyte thermodynamic system.
claims.quote: all the fresh and degraded cathodes exhibited similar DSC proﬁles and heat generation, indicating little changes in the thermal stability of the cathode during aging process. Therefore, the evolution of battery TR behavior during the whole life cycle mainly depended on the changes in the exothermic reactions between the anode and electrolyte.

claims.claim: For graphite/NMC cells with EC/DMC/LiPF6 electrolyte, higher amounts of vinylene carbonate additive suppress the evolution of C2H4 during thermal abuse up to 132 °C, suggesting reduced presence of the organic SEI component lithium ethylene dicarbonate (LEDC).
claims.quote: Higher amounts of the additive vinylene carbonate suppress the evolution of C2H4 during thermal abuse, suggesting a reduced presence of the organic SEI component lithium ethylene dicarbonate.

claims.claim: For all tested VC concentrations and formation rates, evolution of CO2, POF3, and H2 during thermal stress testing started near 70 °C to 80 °C.
claims.quote: For all VC concentrations and formation rates, evolution of CO2, POF3 and H2 started near 70 °C to 80 °C.

claims.claim: Cells with high VC content (0.4 vol%) and low formation rate (C/10) exhibited POF3 evolution approximately three times higher than cells under other tested conditions during thermal stress testing.
claims.quote: For high VC concentration with low formation rate, the relative evolved amount of POF3 is three times higher than at other conditions (Fig. 2)

claims.claim: The detected H2 amount during the heating ramp from 60 °C to 132 °C increases for cells formed at higher formation rates (1C) compared to lower formation rates (C/10) for both VC concentrations tested.
claims.quote: The detected H2 amount during the heating ramp increases for cells with higher formation rates for both VC concentrations.

claims.claim: At temperatures around 130 °C, residual VC can trigger cross-polymerization of EC and VC under strong CO2 evolution, potentially causing rapid internal pressure increase leading to cell venting.
claims.quote: Especially, if temperatures of 130 °C are reached, gas evolution may cause a rapid internal pressure increase leading to cell venting.

claims.claim: Short-circuited all-solid-state batteries can reach temperatures significantly higher than conventional Li-ion batteries, which could lead to fire through flammable packaging and/or nearby materials.
claims.quote: We show that short-circuited all-solid-state batteries can reach temperatures signiﬁcantly higher than conventional Li-ion, which could lead to ﬁre through ﬂammable packaging and/or nearby materials.

claims.claim: At a cathode liquid electrolyte volume fraction of 0.1 in the SSB and 0.3 in the LIB, the potential SSB heat release under external heating is about one-third that of the LIB.
claims.quote: At more typical values, 0.3 VF of LE in the LIB cathode/anode and 0.1 VF LE in the SSB cathode, the potential SSB heat release is about one-third that of the LIB.

claims.claim: As SE thickness decreases and energy density increases from the present-day to the theoretical 2 format, the potential temperature rise of the SSB under external heating (Scenario A) increases from 53°C to 415°C due to liquid electrolyte reactions.
claims.quote: as the energy density increases from the present-day through the theoretical 2 format, the potential temperature rise of the SSB undergoing external heating increases from 53(cid:3)C to 415(cid:3)C due to the presence of LE reactions.

claims.claim: In a short-circuit failure scenario, the SSB and ASSB overtake the LIB in potential temperature rise as energy densities increase, because the thermal mass is smaller and LLZO has a relatively low heat capacity.
claims.quote: Of key interest in Figure 4 is the SSB and ASSB overtaking the LIB in potential temperature rise in a short-circuit failure (Scenario B) as energy densities increase... The increased potential temperature rise occurs because the thermal mass is smaller in the SSB and ASSB conﬁgurations and because LLZO has a relatively low heat capacity.

claims.claim: For the SSB under external heating, a heat-release plateau forms when the cathode liquid electrolyte volume fraction exceeds 0.125, at which point all cathode-generated O2 has reacted with the liquid electrolyte.
claims.quote: For the SSB, a plateau forms as the VF edges above 0.125, when all the cathode-generated O2 has reacted with LE; excess LE may be vented.

claims.claim: At discharge rates of 1C, 3C, 5C, and 8C, the cell temperature increases for an NCM 18650 battery are 4°C, 15°C, 22°C, and 38°C, respectively.
claims.quote: the cell temperature increase for the 1C, 3C, 5C, and 8C discharge process are 4 °C, 15 °C, 22 °C and 38 °C, respectively

claims.claim: For the same C-rate, the charge process produces a smaller cell temperature increase than the discharge process.
claims.quote: in general, the charge process has smaller cell temperature increase than that for the discharge process with the same C-rate

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, thermal runaway is always triggered after discharge for a 1.5 Ah 18650 NCM/Graphite cell when the imposed C-rate reaches 13C.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is always triggered after discharge if the imposed C-rate reaches 13C in an adiabatic environment

claims.claim: No thermal runaway occurs during a very high C-rate charge process because the charge operation is terminated by the early-coming of cut-off voltage.
claims.quote: no thermal runaway or even thermal abuse reactions occur during a very high C-rate charge process as the charge operation is terminated by the early-coming of cut-off voltage

claims.claim: A 12C charge with faulty cut-off voltage control under adiabatic conditions leads to thermal runaway at 320 s, which is faster than the 15C discharge case that requires approximately 400 s after discharge for thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the situation of 12C charge battery with a faulty control is by far more dangerous than the high-C rate discharge cases; a steady stream of power input could push the cell to the threshold point of 150 °C in ~130 s, and ~80 s later leading to thermal runaway. The 15C discharge needs ~400 s for the occurrence of thermal runaway after discharge

claims.claim: For NCM811|AG battery with 1M LiPF6 EC/EMC electrolyte, the three characteristic temperatures were T1=65°C (onset of self-heating), T2=218°C (thermal runaway trigger, defined as when temperature rise rate reached 1°C/s), and T3=750°C (maximum temperature).
claims.quote: In this study, the three characteristic temperatures were located at 65 ℃ (T1), 218 ℃ (T2) and 750 ℃ (T3), respectively.

claims.claim: The reaction between cathode and electrolyte was identified as the thermal runaway trigger for NCM811|AG battery, based on DSC showing a sharp exothermic peak at 215°C for the Ca+Ely and Ca+Ely+An samples, which appeared earlier than the cathode-alone peak at ~230°C.
claims.quote: for the Ca +Ely sample, two peaks appeared at 215 ℃ and 230 ℃. And for the Ca +Ely +An sample, a sharp exothermic peak was also detected at 215 ℃. Based on the time order of the reactions, the reaction between cathode and electrolyte was identified as the thermal runaway trigger.

claims.claim: When TPFPB was added to the electrolyte, the oxygen gas release peak from charged NCM811 cathode shifted to a lower temperature of ~150°C compared to ~230°C for cathode with electrolyte alone, because TPFPB acted as a reducing agent that reduced highly reactive oxygen species into oxygen gas at lower temperature.
claims.quote: Big changes were observed when the electrolyte mixed with TPFPB. As shown in Fig. 3e, the oxygen gas peak starts earlier at ~150 ◦C. This behavior is caused by the TPFPB serving as a reduction agent that reduces the highly reactive oxygen species into oxygen gas.

claims.claim: Adding TPFPB to the NCM811 battery electrolyte lowered T2 to 198°C (20°C lower than the 218°C for standard NCM811), indicating that TPFPB addition made the battery more susceptible to thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: The key temperature, T2, was 198 ◦C for B-NCM811, 20 ◦C lower than for NCM811, indicating that the battery was more likely to thermal runaway after TPFPB added.

claims.claim: In the presence of electrolyte, the lattice strain in the NCM811 cathode was released spontaneously by cation migration, phase transformation, and oxygen release, whereas the dry cathode could endure a much greater lattice strain, indicating that electrolyte accelerates phase transition and oxygen release.
claims.quote: the dry cathode lattice can endure a much greater lattice strain, while the strain in the presence of electrolyte is released spontaneously by cation migration, phase transformation and oxygen release. Therefore, the results show that electrolyte has a negative impact on the phase structure stability of cathode, which could accelerate phase transition and oxygen release.

claims.claim: The thermal stability order of cathode materials is LFP > LMO > NCM111 > NCA > LCO, with LFP being the most stable cathode material during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The order of the thermal stability should be LFP>LMO>NCM111>NCA>LCO [63], with the LFP as the most stable cathode material during TR process.

claims.claim: For NCM cathode materials, the enthalpy of decomposition increases with increasing Ni content: Li0.37[Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3]O2 releases 512.5 J/g, while Li0.21[Ni0.85Co0.075Mn0.075]O2 releases 971.5 J/g.
claims.quote: The cathode with larger proportion of Ni in the NCM releases more heat during decomposition.

claims.claim: The initial decomposition of SEI occurs at 80–120°C with an exothermic peak at approximately 100°C, and may begin as low as 57°C though heat generation only becomes detectable at 80°C or higher.
claims.quote: The initial decomposition of SEI occurs at 80~120oC [79], with a peak locates at approximately 100oC [80]. The onset temperature can be lower than 80oC, as Wang et al. [76] reported that the SEI decomposition might start from a temperature as low as 57oC. However, the exothermic heat generation can only become detectable at 80oC or higher in the calorimetry.

claims.claim: The PE separator melts at approximately 130°C and the PP separator melts at approximately 170°C; both melting processes are endothermic with enthalpies of -90 J/g and -190 J/g respectively.
claims.quote: The melting points of the PE and PP separators are approximately 130oC and 170oC [95,127,128,129], respectively. The separator melting is an endothermic process... The enthalpy of the PE and PP separator during melting are -90 J∙g-1 and -190 J∙g-1, respectively

claims.claim: The collapse temperature of a separator with ceramic coating can be as high as 200–260°C, compared to approximately 130°C for PE and 170°C for PP/PE/PP separators without ceramic coating.
claims.quote: The collapse temperature of the separator with ceramic coating can be as high as 200-260oC [70, 129]. The ISC can occur at approximately 130oC (for the PE separator), 170oC (for the PP or PP/PE/PP separator), or as high as 200oC (for the separator with ceramic coating).

claims.claim: In NCM811/SiC cells, the internal temperature during thermal runaway can reach 1192°C, approximately 400°C higher than traditional NCM111-Graphite chemistries, with a TR onset temperature about 25°C lower.
claims.quote: during the TR of large format NCM811-SiC cells, the internal temperature could reach 1192 °C, almost 400 °C larger than the traditional NCM111-Graphite chemistries, with the TR onset temperature Tonset about 25 °C lower

claims.claim: Replacing SiC-electrolyte kinetics with graphite-electrolyte kinetics reduces total heat generation by approximately 29% (318 J) and decreases Tmax by 318°C, representing the largest single thermal contribution among the substitutions tested.
claims.quote: The 'Graphite-Ele' kinetic replacement shows the most obvious influence, and reduces the total heat by about 29% (318 J). While 'NCM111-Graphite', 'NCM111-Ele' kinetic substitutions result in heat decrease by 17% and 20%, respectively.

claims.claim: Replacing anode-electrolyte kinetics with graphite-electrolyte kinetics raises the TR onset temperature Tonset by 11.7% (from 179°C to 200°C) and reduces Tmax by 27.1%, while cathode-anode and cathode-electrolyte substitutions show no change in Tonset.

Replacing cathode-anode (NCM811-SiC) kinetics with NCM111-graphite kinetics reduces (dT/dt)max by 93.6%, indicating that cathode-anode interactions are the dominant driver of maximum temperature rising rate during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: (dT/dt)max is mainly influenced by the 'Ca-An' interactions ... Ca-An ... Difference ... −93.6%

claims.claim: The maximum internal temperature during thermal runaway of the 74 Ah NCM811/SiC cell was 490°C higher than the maximum surface temperature (1192°C internal vs. 702°C surface), and the internal (dT/dt)max was almost 20 times the surface (dT/dt)max.
claims.quote: The max temperature Tmax inside is 490 °C larger than the Tmax outside, while the internal temperature rising rate (dT/dt)max is almost 20 times the value of the (dT/dt)max outside.

claims.claim: The internal gas pressure at the onset of top venting was recorded as approximately 19.85 bar, 19.96 bar, and 19.84 bar in Tests #1 to #3 respectively for 21700-format NCA cells at approximately 95–99% SOC.
claims.quote: The internal pressure at the onset of top venting was recorded as 19.85 bar, 19.96 bar and 19.84 bar in Tests #1 to #3 respectively.

claims.claim: When the top vent activated, the internal gas pressure within the cell rapidly reduced to 1 bar, and the internal cell temperature reduced to approximately 55°C due to the release of hot gases.
claims.quote: When the top vent activates, the internal gas pressure within the cell rapidly reduced to 1 bar, whereas internal cell temperature reduces to circa 55°C. The temperature decrease is caused by the release of hot gasses from the cell.

claims.claim: In Test #3, the bottom vent did not activate during thermal runaway, which restricted gas release and led to a significantly higher secondary internal gas pressure peak of 28.74 bar compared to 4.54 bar and 4.19 bar in Tests #1 and #2 where the bottom vent was active.
claims.quote: However, the bottom vent in Test #3 did not activate during thermal runaway. This restricted the gas release, leading to a significantly higher internal gas pressure in Test #3 (secondary pulse nearly 30 bar).

claims.claim: In Test #3, where the bottom vent did not activate, the cell mass decreased dramatically from 67.37 g to 12.78 g after thermal runaway, compared to 28.45 g and 27.46 g in Tests #1 and #2 where the bottom vent was active.
claims.quote: the cell mass decreased from 67.37 g to 28.45 g in Test #1, 27.46 g in Test #2, and dramatically to 12.78 g in Test #3.

claims.claim: A higher SOC corresponded to a lower thermal runaway onset temperature: the cell at 99% SOC had an onset temperature of 166°C, the cell at 97% SOC had an onset temperature of 178°C, and the cell at 95% SOC had an onset temperature of 195°C.
claims.quote: According to surface cell measurements, the cell in Test #3, with a SOC of 99 %, exhibited the lowest onset temperature of 166°C. This was followed by the cell in Test #1, with a SOC of 97 %, which had an onset temperature of 178°C, and the cell in Test #2, with a SOC of 95 %, which had an onset temperature of 195°C.

claims.claim: The average thermal runaway propagation (TRP) velocity under side heating (2.035 ± 0.058 mm/s) and bottom heating (1.942 ± 0.217 mm/s) is approximately seven times greater than under large surface heating (0.296 ± 0.007 mm/s).
claims.quote: the values of νTR under SH and BH mode are 2.035 ± 0.058 mm/s and 1.942 ± 0.217 mm/s, indicating the TR speed under these two heat transfer paths is comparable. The νTR value of the LSH mode is determined to be 0.296 ± 0.007 mm/s, which is only about one-seventh of that of side and bottom heating mode.

claims.claim: The thermal conductivity of the LFP battery perpendicular to the electrode plane (λx = 0.8348 W/m K) is approximately twenty times lower than the thermal conductivity parallel to the electrode plane (λy = λz = 18.034 W/m K).
claims.quote: the values of λx and λy (λz) of is 0.8348 W/m K and18.034 W/m K

claims.claim: Under large surface heating, voltage drop occurs at the end of the internal TRP stage, whereas under side and bottom heating, voltage drop occurs at the initial stage of internal TRP due to almost simultaneous internal short circuit of all core layers.
claims.quote: the voltage drop under large surface heating occurs at the end of internal TRP stage, while voltage drops under side and bottom heating is observed at the initial stage of internal TRP

claims.claim: Compared with large surface heating, batteries under side and bottom heating exhibit higher overall temperature, higher peak temperature and temperature increment of the large surface, and less mass loss during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Compared with large surface heating, batteries under side and bottom heating exhibits higher overall temperature, peak temperature and temperature increment of large surface and less mass loss.

claims.claim: Under large surface heating, the gas volume produced during thermal runaway (712.78 ± 6.61 L) is more than twice that produced under side heating (330.62 ± 16.34 L) and bottom heating (342.11 ± 3.69 L).
claims.quote: The gas volume under SH and BH mode are comparable, and their values are 330.62 ± 16.34 L and 342.11 ± 3.69 L, respectively. The volume of the gas generated in the process of TR under LSH mode is more than twice that of SH and BH mode, and its value is 712.78 ± 6.61 L.

claims.claim: Lithium dendrite growth induced by inhomogeneous current density distribution on the lithium metal anode may pierce the polymer separator, resulting in short circuit and subsequent thermal runaway of the cell.
claims.quote: The formation and subsequent growth of lithium dendrites induced by inhomogeneous distribution of current density on the lithium metal anode may pierce the polymer separator, resulting in short circuit and subsequent thermal runaway of the cell.

claims.claim: In symmetric Li/Li cells with pristine lithium exposed to LiPS-rich electrolyte, the real part of impedance increased rapidly, reaching approximately 250% of the initial value after 72 hours, compared to only 45% increase for AlI3-treated lithium under the same conditions.
claims.quote: the real part of the impedance of the control cells is seen to increase rapidly, reaching a value of around 250% of the initial value after 72 hours. In contrast, cells containing protected lithium are noticeably less reactive when in contact with LiPS rich electrolyte—only 45% increase is observed for the impedance over 72h.

claims.claim: When current density was increased to 2 mA/cm2, AlI3 pretreatment of lithium metal increased Coulombic efficiency from approximately 70% to approximately 92%.
claims.quote: the improvement is still observed when the current density is increased to 2 mA/cm2 (Figure 2c), where the efficiency is increased from ~70% to ~ 92% when lithium metal is pretreated with AlI3.

claims.claim: When AlI3 is additionally introduced to the Li-S cell electrolyte beyond the pretreatment step, both capacity and Coulombic efficiency rise, with CE exceeding 97% by the 100th cycle at 0.5C.
claims.quote: when additional AlI3 is introduced to the electrolyte, both the capacity and efficiency rise, with the CE exceeding 97% by the 100th cycle.

claims.claim: High-order lithium polysulfides Li2Sn with n greater than 2 dissolve in aprotic liquid electrolyte solvents, causing low utilization of active materials and parasitic reaction with the Li anode, whereas low-order Li2Sn with n ≤ 2 are insoluble.
claims.quote: Whereas the low order Li2Sn (n ≤ 2) are insoluble in most aprotic liquid electrolyte solvents, high order lithium polysulfides Li2Sn (n > 2) dissolve, causing low utilization of active materials and parasitic reaction with the Li anode.

claims.claim: When the nail diameter was increased to 12 mm and completely inserted into the 280 Ah LFP battery, thermal runaway occurred and the maximum temperature increased from 84 to 384.5 °C.
claims.quote: When the diameter of the nail was increased to 12 mm, the battery experienced TR and the maximum temperature increased from 84 to 384.5 (cid:3)C.

claims.claim: By controlling heat dissipation conditions (thickening insulation cotton), the time interval between two thermal runaway events in the 280 Ah LFP battery was delayed from a minimum of 558 s to 1417 s.
claims.quote: After the insulation cotton was thickened, the interval between thermal runaway of two jelly rolls was delayed from minimum of 558 to 1417 s.

claims.claim: In the absence of combustion during the second thermal runaway event, the concentration peaks of the four generated gases (CO, CO2, CH4, C2H4) were significantly lower than those of the first thermal runaway event.
claims.quote: in the absence of combustion, the concentration peaks of the four generated gases brought on by the second TR were signiﬁcantly lower than those of the ﬁrst TR

claims.claim: When only a single jelly roll experienced thermal runaway in the high-capacity LFP battery, the voltage remained above 3100 mV after a small fluctuation until the second jelly roll completed thermal runaway.
claims.quote: only a single jelly roll experienced thermal runaway, and the voltage remained above 3100 mV after a small ﬂuctuation until the second jelly roll completed thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway did not occur in the nail penetration experiment of the high-capacity LFP battery when the separator damaged area was too small or heat dissipation was excessive, preventing the generated heat from further dissolving the separator.
claims.quote: TR would not occur in the nail penetration experiment of the high-capacity LFP battery if the separator damaged area was too small or the heat dissipation was excessive, preventing the generated heat from further dissolving the separator.

claims.claim: The trigger temperature of thermal runaway (T2) for Gr|NMC811 battery with concentrated LiFSI/DMC electrolyte was 200.5 °C, which is 12.6 °C lower than the 213.1 °C observed with conventional 1 M LiPF6/EC:EMC electrolyte.
claims.quote: T2 was located at 200.5 °C. A fall in the OCV took place at T2, which coincides with the sharp temperature rise. However, T2 of the cell with the conventional electrolyte reached 213.1 °C and simultaneously with OCV falling (see Fig. 2b), which is 12.6 °C higher than that of the case with the concentrated electrolyte.

claims.claim: The mixture of lithiated anode (An) and LiFSI exhibited a sharp exothermic peak of 602.9 J g−1 at 209.6 °C, which was eight times greater than the heat released by the anode alone (~70 J g−1 at ~289 °C).
claims.quote: for the mixture of LiFSI and An, when the lithiated anode was in contact with the concentrated LiFSI/DMC electrolyte, the sample showed a sharp exothermic peak (602.9 J g−1) at 209.6 °C, which was eight times greater than that of the anode alone

claims.claim: The CaEly partial cell (cathode + concentrated LiFSI/DMC electrolyte) did not enter thermal runaway; its maximum dT/dt was lower than 0.1 °C s−1 and T3 was 290 °C, indicating that cathode-electrolyte reactions were incapable of triggering thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the CaEly partial cell did not enter thermal runaway. No sharp temperature rise was observed from the ARC curve, and its T3 was 290 °C. Meanwhile, the maximum dT/dt was even lower than 0.1 °C s−1, which is far lower than 1 °C s−1. These results illustrate that the heat generated by the reactions inside the CaEly partial cell, including the cathode material decomposition and electrolyte oxidation by the cathode material, was relatively small before 290 °C and incapable of triggering the battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The Gr|NMC811 battery with non-flammable concentrated LiFSI/TMP electrolyte was brought to thermal runaway at T2 of 195.2 °C, which is lower than the T2 of 200.5 °C observed with the LiFSI/DMC concentrated electrolyte.
claims.quote: the battery was brought to thermal runaway at a T2 of 195.2 °C (Fig. 6a), which is even less than the T2 of the battery with the LiFSI/DMC concentrated electrolyte.

claims.claim: The flammability of an electrolyte reflects its behavior when oxidized by oxygen, whereas it is the electrolyte reduction by the lithiated anode that triggers the chain of exothermic reactions leading to thermal runaway in LiFSI-based concentrated electrolyte batteries.
claims.quote: the flammability of an electrolyte represents its behaviors when oxidized by oxygen, while it is the electrolyte reduction that triggers the chain of exothermic reactions in a battery. Thus, this study lights the way to a deeper understanding of the thermal runaway mechanism in batteries as well as the design philosophy of electrolytes for safer lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: In a LiB with NMC/Graphite electrodes and PE-based ceramic coated separator, as temperature increases, SEI/CEI decomposes first at 60–120°C, followed by PE separator thermal shrinkage or melting at ~135°C, NMC cathode decomposition at ~200°C, and electrolyte decomposition at 250–300°C.
claims.quote: As temperature increases, the SEI/CEI decomposes first (60 ~ 120°C), followed by the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte, the thermal shrinkage or melting of the PE separator at ~135°C, the decomposition of NMC cathode (~200°C), and the decomposition of electrolyte (250 ~ 300°C).

claims.claim: LiPF6 decomposes into LiF and PF5 at temperatures as low as ~85°C in the presence of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC).
claims.quote: LiPF6 is thermally unstable and decomposes into LiF and PF5, which occurs at temperature as low as ~85°C in the presence of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC).

claims.claim: SEI formed with acidic salts LiPF6 and LiBF4 has low thermal stability, with decomposition starting at about 60°C with LiBF4 addition and 80°C with LiPF6 addition.
claims.quote: SEI of low thermal stability forms on the graphite surface when the acidic LiPF6 and LiBF4 salts are involved; this SEI is rich in LiF resulting from decomposition of the lithium salt and lithium alkylcarbonate. The decomposition starts at about 60°C with the addition of LiBF4 and 80°C with the addition of LiPF6, respectively.

claims.claim: Using thermally stable lithium salts LiCF3SO3, LiTFSI, and LiBOB instead of LiPF6 and LiBF4 pushes the onset temperature of SEI decomposition to 110–135°C.
claims.quote: SEI of higher thermal stability and composed of inorganic CF3CF2Li, Li2C2O4, LixSOy, Li2S, Li3N, LiF, and B2O3 forms when the thermally-stable lithium salts of LiCF3SO3, LiN(SO2CF3)2 (LiTFSI), and bis(oxalato)borate (LiBOB) are used instead of LiPF6 and LiBF4, pushing Tonset of SEI decomposition to 110–135°C.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway onset temperature of Li/LiCoO2 pouch cells using LiTFSI-LiDFOB-LiPF6 electrolytes (~181°C) is higher than that using 1 M LiPF6 electrolyte (~143°C) as measured by ARC tests.
claims.quote: according to ARC tests of the Li/LiCoO2 pouch cells (Figure 2B), thermal runaway Tonset of cells using LiTFSI-LiDFOB-LiPF6 electrolytes (~181°C) is higher than that using 1 M LiPF6 electrolyte (~143°C).

claims.claim: During 3C discharge to 0.5 V, the temperature rise rate increases, then decreases, then increases again, because early-stage heat generation is dominated by Joule heat and polarization heat, while late-stage side-reaction heat generation rises sharply and becomes dominant.
claims.quote: the rate of temperature rise of the battery increases and then decreases, and then increases again, which is due to the fact that the heat generation in the early stage of discharge is mainly Joule heat and polarization heat, and the heat generation of the side reaction in the late stage of discharge rises sharply and occupies the dominant position

claims.claim: Under 10C discharge conditions, copper current collector dissolution and subsequent deposition on cathode surfaces occurred.
claims.quote: copper current collector dissolution and subsequent deposition on cathode surfaces emerged under 10C discharge conditions

claims.claim: Batteries after 6C and 10C discharges to 0.5 V suffered irreversible damage to anode and cathode material structures, leading to battery failure with discharge capacity reduced to zero.
claims.quote: The batteries after 6C and 10C discharges caused irreversible damage to the structure of the battery anode and cathode materials, leading to battery failure

claims.claim: At the same discharge cutoff voltage of 0.5 V, the average maximum surface temperatures for 3C, 6C, and 10C discharges were 81.504°C, 123.864°C, and 130.31°C respectively, showing a nonlinear increase with discharge rate.
claims.quote: the average maximum temperature of 3C, 6C and 10C discharged to 0.5 V is 81.504°C, 123.864°C and 130.31°C, respectively, in which the temperature difference between 3C and 6C increases much more than that between 6C and 10C, and the rate of heat production is nonlinearly correlated with the multiplicity

claims.claim: Discharge capacity decreased with increasing discharge rate: 1917.2 mAh at 3C, 1638.2 mAh at 6C, and 851 mAh at 10C, all discharged to 0.5 V.
claims.quote: The discharge capacity is 1917.2 mAh when the discharge multiplier is 3C, decreases to 1638.2 mAh at 6C discharge multiplier, and the discharge capacity is 851 mAh at 10C discharge multiplier

claims.claim: Cells with higher SOC are easier to be triggered into thermal runaway during nail penetration and obtain higher peak temperatures.
claims.quote: The results show that the cells with higher SOC and penetrated by bigger nail are easier to be triggered into thermal runaway and obtain higher temperature.

claims.claim: During nail penetration experiments at 100% SOC with a 3-mm nail at 20 mm/s, the voltage dropped from 4.18 V to 0.23 V at the moment of penetration.
claims.quote: At the moment the nail penetrated into the cell at 50 s, the voltage of the pouch cell dropped from 4.18 to 0.23 V and then it fluctuated slightly and reached a peak of 0.4 V at 62.5 s.

claims.claim: The temperature near the nail penetration site (T3) began to rise first, while temperatures at cathode (T1), anode (T2), and bottom (T4) positions lagged by 0.6 s, 0.6 s, and 0.8 s respectively.
claims.quote: The time to start heating up of T1, T2 and T4 was 0.6 s, 0.6 s and 0.8 s, respectively, which is slower than the time to start heating up of T3.

claims.claim: The peak temperature of the cell at the nail penetration site was not the highest; instead, a lower temperature area appeared around the penetration site because part of the heat was conducted away through the nail remaining in the cell.
claims.quote: the temperature of T3, which had the maximum initial temperature rise rate, was not the maximum temperature of the entire cell, while the maximum temperature emerged at the cathode position. This was mainly because the nail was kept in the cell after it was inserted. Part of the heat was conducted away through the nail, resulting in lower temperature in the middle position.

claims.claim: Penetration speed (10–40 mm/s) had little effect on the peak temperature of the cell during nail penetration, with Tavg values all approximately 373.1 K across all tested speeds.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of the cell with different penetration speeds was similar, which was about 371.1–378.6 K. The Tavg of the cells with different penetration speeds is listed in Table 3. The Tavg was all around 373.1 K. Therefore, the penetration speed had little effect on the temperature of the cell.

claims.claim: A larger nail diameter caused a higher initial temperature rise rate and higher peak temperature: the 8-mm nail produced a peak temperature of 381.7 K, compared to 377.1 K for 5 mm and 376.8 K for 3 mm.
claims.quote: The peak temperature was the highest for 8-mm-diameter nail, which could be up to 381.7 K, while that for 5 mm and 3 mm was 377.1 K and 376.8 K, respectively.

claims.claim: The peak temperature of all test cells was below 403.1 K, so the separator did not melt, and only SEI decomposition and the reaction between anode electrode materials and electrolyte occurred during nail penetration.
claims.quote: The peak temperature of all test cells was below 403.1 K. Besides, Fig. 13 shows the morphology of separator after nail penetration. It can be seen that the separator did not shrink or melt, except for being penetrated. Hence, in this study, only SEI decomposition and the reaction between anode electrode materials and electrolyte occurred.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition occurs in the temperature range of 363.1–393.1 K, and the reaction between anode electrode materials and electrolyte proceeds at approximately 373.1 K.
claims.quote: the decomposition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) (363.1–393.1 K), the reaction between anode electrode materials and electrolyte (373.1 K), thermal decomposition of electrolytes (473.1–573.1 K)

claims.claim: When T_E is less than T_M (warning zone), heat generation exceeds heat removal (Q_g > Q_r), and thermal runaway becomes unstoppable.
claims.quote: T_E < T_M is denoted the warning zone at which Q_g > Q_r as illustrated in Figs. 9(a), (f), and 10(g), (h). When a system arrives at this warning zone, the TR is unstoppable.

claims.claim: Beyond the equilibrium temperature T_E, the rate of heat generation is higher than the rate of heat removal, resulting in self-sustaining reactions and auto-ignition of active materials, making it impossible to limit temperature rise by external measures.
claims.quote: Beyond this point, the rate of heat generation is higher than the rate of heat removal, resulting in self-sustaining reactions, which means there is auto-ignition of active materials occurring within the LIB system, and it is no longer possible to limit the temperature rise by external measures beyond this point because dQ_g/dT > dQ_r/dT.

claims.claim: At a heating rate of 5 °C/min, the heat of reaction for R I was 447.95 J/g for NCA91/SCN and 178.61 J/g for NCA88/SCN, indicating that lower heating rates cause more severe anode side reactions in ultrahigh-nickel LIBs.
claims.quote: the calculated heat of reaction for NCA88/SCN and NCA91/SCN were 178.61 J/g and 447.95 J/g, respectively as observed from Fig. 6(a). This implies that at lower heating rates, the anode side reactions can occur severely in ultrahigh-nickel LIBs.

claims.claim: For heating rates greater than 10 °C/min, Reaction II is the triggering reaction leading to thermal runaway if the heat removal rate is insufficient, whereas thermal runaway is least likely to occur under slow heating at 5 °C/min.
claims.quote: for higher heating rates (i.e. > 10 °C/min), R II is the triggering reaction which leads to TR if the heat removal rate is insufficient in the battery system... unlike fast heating (10–20 °C/min), the TR in ultrahigh capacity LIBs is least likely to occur under slow heating (5 °C/min), because the rate of heat dissipation is higher than the internal heat generation.

claims.claim: A 3% increase in Ni content in the cathode material (from NCA88 to NCA91) caused the onset of thermal runaway to occur 15% earlier in reaction progress at SOC 100%.
claims.quote: NCA91 initiated the TR at 0.65 of α, while it was 0.80 for NCA88, indicating that only a 3% increase in Ni content of the cathode material can precede the onset of TR by 15% of reaction progress at SOC 100%.

claims.claim: The induction period before thermal runaway is inversely proportional to SOC level, and is much shorter (1–2 min) under rapid heating rates of 10–20 °C/min compared to lower heating rates.
claims.quote: the induction period is inversely proportional to SOC levels for LIBs. Besides, it is highly dependent on the heating condition, i.e. for higher heating rates (10–20 °C/min), the 'high-alert' zone is very narrow as compared to the lower heating rates (5 °C/min). This signifies that under rapid heating, the high-alert period is much shorter (1–2 min).

claims.claim: Lithium methyl carbonate (LMC) accounts for more than 50% of the SEI layer due to the use of ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) electrolyte.
claims.quote: Among the ROCOOLi compounds, lithium methyl carbonate (LMC) accounts for more than 50 % of the SEI layer because of the use of EMC

claims.claim: Thermal runaway begins with battery self-heating at approximately 100°C, where SEI layer components undergo thermal decomposition reactions causing gas evolution and heat generation.
claims.quote: A typical thermal runaway begins with the battery self-heating at about 100 C, the temperature where various components of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer on the anode surface undergo thermal decomposition reactions, which is a source of gas evolution and heat generation

claims.claim: The SEI layer has a total thickness of 10–50 nm and comprises organic materials in the inner layer and inorganic materials in the outer layer.
claims.quote: The SEI layer is a thin layer that has a total thickness of 10–50 nm and comprises various organic (inner layer) and inorganic materials (outer layer) on the surface of the anode active material

claims.claim: An unstable organic SEI layer decomposes into an inorganic SEI layer when exposed to thermal load, driven mainly by the decomposition of lithium alkyl carbonate (ROCOOLi).
claims.quote: An unstable organic SEI layer decomposes into an inorganic SEI layer when exposed to thermal load because of its low stability during heat and gas generation, which is mainly driven by the decomposition of lithium alkyl carbonate (ROCOOLi)

claims.claim: The decomposition reaction of LMC alone is endothermic, but when mixed with electrolyte, an exothermic reaction occurs around 100°C, which corresponds to the self-heating stage.
claims.quote: the decomposition reaction of LMC is endothermic, but when mixed with the electrolyte, an exothermic reaction occurs around 100 C, which is previously reported as the self-heating stage

claims.claim: Fluorinated electrolytes improved the thermal runaway triggering temperature T2 by nearly 20 °C compared to conventional carbonate electrolyte in NMC811|Gr cells.
claims.quote: T2 was 206.2 °C, 224.3 °C, and 225.6 °C for the NMC811|Gr-EC + DMC, NMC811|Gr-FEC + FEMC, and NMC811|Gr-FEC + D2 cells, respectively. The fluorinated electrolytes improved T2 by nearly 20 °C.

claims.claim: All NMC811|Gr cells with different electrolytes (conventional carbonate and fluorinated) reached a maximum temperature T3 of approximately 770 °C and released approximately -13.4 kJ of heat during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: All cells showed T3 of around 770 °C and the heat released by the cell materials was approximately −13.4 kJ (Equation S1 and Table S4) for the three cells, although they follow different heat accumulation pathways caused by the difference in electrolyte ingredient.
