claims.claim: The CaFely partial cell (cathode + fluorinated electrolyte of 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/D2) did not enter thermal runaway, with a maximum dT/dt of only 0.45 °C/s, consistent with the superior oxidation resistance of the fluorinated electrolyte against the cathode.
claims.quote: The CaFely partial cell did not enter thermal runaway, and its maximum dT∕dt−1 was only 0.45 °C s−1. This result complies with the superior oxidation resistance of the fluorinated electrolyte against the cathode.

claims.claim: The reactions of LiC6 + LiPF6 and LiC6 + FEC account for 13% of total heat during thermal runaway of NMC811|Gr cells with fluorinated electrolyte, which is sufficient to trigger chain reactions leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The reactions for LiC6 + LiPF6 and LiC6 + FEC pioneer with a heat share of 13%, providing sufficient accumulated heat in the self-heating stage. The heat accumulation further triggers the battery into thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The cross-talk reaction between cathode-releasing oxygen and the anode (Ca + An) accounts for 66% of the total heat during thermal runaway of NMC811|Gr cells with fluorinated electrolyte, making it the dominant heat-generating reaction after thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: The intensive cross-talk reaction between the cathode-releasing oxygen and anode is initiated (Peak V), as shown in Figure 4a. The reaction of Ca + An occupies 66% of the total heat, which is the major reaction after the cell undergoes thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The perfluorinated electrolyte increased the thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 by 12.5 °C compared to the conventional electrolyte in SC-NCM811|Gr cells.
claims.quote: for the battery using a perfluorinated electrolyte, T2 increased by 12.5 °C and T3 decreased by 41.2 °C

claims.claim: The perfluorinated electrolyte decreased the highest thermal runaway temperature T3 by 41.2 °C compared to the conventional electrolyte in SC-NCM811|Gr cells.
claims.quote: for the battery using a perfluorinated electrolyte, T2 increased by 12.5 °C and T3 decreased by 41.2 °C

claims.claim: The SC-NCM811|Gr cell with conventional electrolyte reached a thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 of 198.7 °C and a maximum temperature T3 of 692.8 °C.
claims.quote: The SC-NCM811∣Gr battery with conventional electrolyte T2 was located at 198.7 °C... After a few seconds, the battery reached the highest temperature T3 (692.8 °C).

claims.claim: The combination of cathode and conventional electrolyte produced an exothermic peak at 224.7 °C with an enthalpy change of 172.2 J/g, while the combination with perfluorinated electrolyte produced an exothermic peak near 200 °C with an enthalpy of only 83.85 J/g, approximately half that of the conventional electrolyte.
claims.quote: The combination of the cathode and conventional electrolyte produced a sharp exothermic peak at 224.7 °C, with an enthalpy change (ΔH) of 172.2 J·g−1... The combination of the cathode and perfluorinated electrolyte also produced an exothermic peak at approximately 200 °C, but the reaction enthalpy was half that of the conventional electrolyte, 83.85 J·g−1.

claims.claim: The use of a conventional electrolyte advanced the oxygen production temperature by approximately 50 °C and released a large amount of oxygen gas compared to the pure cathode, indicating that the conventional electrolyte accelerates the rate of the cathodic phase transition reaction.
claims.quote: The use of a conventional electrolyte would advance the oxygen production temperature by ∼50 °C and release a large amount of oxygen gas, indicating that the conventional electrolyte would accelerate the rate of the cathodic reaction and phase transition.

claims.claim: No CO2 emission was observed from the cathode and perfluorinated electrolyte combination during the exothermic process, confirming the absence of a redox reaction between the perfluorinated electrolyte and cathode.
claims.quote: CO2 emission was not observed in the cathode and perfluorinated electrolyte during the exothermic process, confirming that there was no redox reaction between the perfluorinated electrolyte and cathode.

claims.claim: The CEI layer formed on the cathode cycled in the perfluorinated electrolyte was 8 nm thick and uniform, whereas the CEI layer formed in the conventional electrolyte was 20 nm thick and uneven with visible impurities after 200 cycles.
claims.quote: a dense, uniform, and clean CEI layer (8 nm) was formed on the surface of the cathode circulating in the perfluorinated electrolyte (Fig. 4b). In contrast, the CEI layer formed on the surface of the cathode circulating in the conventional electrolyte (Fig. 4a) is 20 nm thick and uneven with visible impurities.

claims.claim: Particle cracking in the cathode causes the new cathode surface to contact the electrolyte, resulting in intensified side reactions, increased CEI layer thickness, and reduced thermal stability and thermal safety of the battery.
claims.quote: Particle cracking could cause the new cathode surface to contact the electrolyte, resulting in intensified side reactions, increased CEI layer thickness, and reduced thermal stability and thermal safety of the battery

claims.claim: The capacity retention rate of the SC-NCM811|Gr cell cycled in the perfluorinated electrolyte was 110.12% after 200 cycles, while the cell cycled in the conventional electrolyte had a capacity retention rate of only 89.80% after 200 cycles.
claims.quote: the capacity retention rate of the perfluorinated electrolyte-based cell was as high as 110.12% after 200 cycles... the capacity of the battery based on the conventional electrolyte began to decline after approximately 50 cycles (Fig. 1c), and the capacity retention rate was only 89.80% after 200 cycles.

claims.claim: When the discharge rate increased from 1C to 4C, the discharge capacity of the cell using conventional electrolyte decreased from 842 to 42 mAh, while the cell using perfluorinated electrolyte decreased from 893 to 429 mAh.
claims.quote: when the multiplication rate increased from 1 C to 4 C, the discharge capacity of the cell using conventional electrolyte decreased from 842 to 42 mAh, while the discharge capacity of the battery using perfluorinated electrolyte only decreased from 893 to 429 mAh.

claims.claim: At 30% SOC and 2 mm/s nail penetration speed, the voltage exhibits a stair-step decrease with increasing penetration depth, with the maximum voltage drop of 1.53 V occurring in the first unit cell.
claims.quote: Comparing the voltage changes in 5 unit cells, the maximum voltage drop (1.53 V) occurs in the first unit cell. As the number of units increases, the voltage drop tends to decrease.

claims.claim: When the nail punctures the first unit cell at 30% SOC and 2 mm/s, the short-circuit current surges rapidly to approximately 47 A; as the nail advances into the second unit cell, the total short-circuit current increases from 47 A to 60 A with a lower increment than the first short-circuit event.
claims.quote: As the nail pierces the first unit cell, it forms a loop with short-circuit current Is-1. At this point, all non-short-circuited unit cells discharge through the short-circuit point of the first unit cell, leading to a rapid surge in short-circuit current to about 47 A. When the nail advances into the second unit cell (d > 0.354 mm), discharge occurs through both short-circuit points, resulting in an increase in Is from 47 A to 60 A, with a lower increment than the preceding short-circuit.

claims.claim: At 90% SOC with 2 mm nail radius and 2 mm/s penetration speed, the maximum voltage drop can reach approximately 1.9 V and the temperature can rise 800°C, compared to approximately 1.5 V voltage drop and lower temperature rise at 30% SOC.
claims.quote: For the 90 % SOC case, the voltage drop during first ISC can reach approximately 2 V, while for 30 % SOC, it is around 1.5 V... The cell experiences a maximum temperature rise of 820 °C as the SOC increases from 30 % to 90 %.

claims.claim: As nail radius increases from 1.2 mm to 3.5 mm at 30% SOC and 2 mm/s, the maximum temperature rises from 300°C to over 400°C, due to the enlarged short-circuit area generating higher short-circuit current and increased Joule heating.
claims.quote: as the penetration radius increases from 1.2 mm to 3.5 mm, the maximum temperature rises from 300 °C to over 400 °C, showcasing a faster temperature escalation trend. This phenomenon can be attributed to the enlarged radius of the nail, which results in a larger short-circuit area and generates a heightened short-circuit current

claims.claim: At a slow puncture speed of 0.5 mm/s, the temperature peak reaches approximately 800°C and the first unit cell experiences a short-circuit current up to 60 A, which is significantly higher than at speeds of 2.0 mm/s or greater where temperature variation trends remain consistent.
claims.quote: at a speed of 0.5 mm/s, the temperature peak is notably higher than at other puncture speeds, reaching about 800 °C. However, for speeds of v ≥ 2.0 mm/s, the temperature variation trend of the cell remains consistent... at v = 0.5 mm/s, the first unit cell experiences a current value of up to 60 A, which is significantly higher than that of other puncture speeds.

claims.claim: HS-LIB with 100% SOC has a self-heating onset temperature (Tonset) of 78°C and a thermal runaway triggering temperature (Ttr) of 138°C, which are lower than those of traditional LIBs.
claims.quote: Poor thermal safety is identified by the lower self-heating onset temperature Tonset (78◦C) and thermal runaway triggering temperature Ttr (138◦C) of HS-LIB with 100 % state of charge (SOC)

claims.claim: HS-LIB with 100% SOC reaches a maximum temperature exceeding 1200°C during thermal runaway, while most traditional LIBs show a Tmax of less than 1000°C.
claims.quote: The Tmax is more than 1200◦C, while most traditional LIBs show a Tmax value of less than 1000◦C.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dtmax) of HS-LIB with 100% SOC is 349.3°C/s, which is 2–3 times higher than those of comparable LIBs.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dtmax) (349.3◦C/s) is 2–3 times higher than those of LIBs in Table S2

claims.claim: Significant volume changes of SiOx anode material contribute to uneven SEI formation, and the resultant cracks and voids accelerate SEI decomposition prematurely, lowering the thermal stability of HS-LIBs.
claims.quote: The significant volume changes of SiOx as an anode material contribute to uneven SEI formation, while the resultant cracks and voids accelerate SEI decomposition prematurely.

claims.claim: Under external overheating at 500 W, HS-LIB with 100% SOC exhibits a thermal runaway triggering temperature (Ttr) of 168°C, a time to thermal runaway (ttr) of 177 s, and a maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dtmax) of 933.8°C/s.
claims.quote: External overheating conditions also results in a lower Ttr (168◦C) and shorter time to thermal runaway ttr (177 s) due to faster heat absorption with dT/dtmax (933.8◦C/s).

claims.claim: For the 38 Ah NCM battery at 100% SOC under EV+ ARC, the onset exothermic temperature (Tonset) is 76 °C, the thermal runaway trigger temperature (TTR) is 188 °C, and the maximum temperature (Tmax) is 417 °C.
claims.quote: Figure 1a provides the following parameters: Tonset is 76 °C, TTR is 188 °C, and Tmax is 417 °C.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway occurs in the 38 Ah NCM battery when the SOC exceeds 35%; batteries at 0% and 25% SOC do not undergo thermal runaway.
claims.quote: TR occurs when the battery exceeds 35%SOC.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the Tonset and TTR of the 38 Ah battery decrease while Tmax increases, indicating lower safety at higher SOC.
claims.quote: With the increase of SOC state, the Tonset and TTR of the battery decreases, while the Tmax increases, indicating that the higher the SOC of the battery, the more unsafe of the battery.

claims.claim: The swelling rate of the 38 Ah battery after thermal runaway increases with SOC, ranging from 91.28% at 0% SOC to 117.19% at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The swelling rate of the different SOCs is between the range from 91.28 to 117.19%. Higher SOC battery has larger swelling rate, indicating the more severe reaction in the high SOC battery.

claims.claim: The cathode electrode alone undergoes thermal runaway at 189 °C and the anode electrode alone undergoes thermal runaway at approximately 240 °C, demonstrating that internal short circuit is not the major heat source during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The cathode electrode enters into an exothermic range at about 158 °C... the TR of the cathode at 189 °C. The anode electrode enters into exothermic range at about 143 °C... TR of the anode electrode at about 240 °C. These results indicate that only the cathode or anode electrode can also occur TR. Therefore, the ISC is not the major heat source during TR.

claims.claim: The SEI film decomposition begins at 100–130 °C with a peak at approximately 110 °C, and the balanced reaction of SEI decomposition and regeneration is not broken until the temperature rises to 270 °C or higher, above which graphite anode reacts with electrolyte and binder.
claims.quote: The initial decomposition of SEI occurs at 100–130 °C with a peak located at approximately 110 °C. Within the temperature range of 100–270 °C, the SEI decomposition and regeneration occurs simultaneously. The balanced reaction of the SEI decomposition and regeneration will not be broken until the temperature rises to 270 °C or higher. Above that temperature, graphite anode reacts with the electrolyte and binder.

claims.claim: The total heat generation from DSC material analysis is 371,087 J, while the total heat generation during thermal runaway calculated from ARC results is 314,296 J; the ARC value is lower because ARC measures surface temperature while the inner temperature is higher.
claims.quote: According to the DSC results, the total heat generation of materials is 371,087 J, while the total heat generation during TR calculated from ARC results is 314,296 J. The value calculated from ARC results is lower than DSC results. That may be because the temperature obtained from ARC results is the surface temperature of the battery. The inner temperature may be higher than the surface temperature.

claims.claim: When the cathode material is heated to 300 °C, a structural phase transition from the rhombohedral phase to the spinel phase occurs, and after heating to 400 °C, another phase transition from LiM2O4-type spinel to Co3O4-type spinel occurs.
claims.quote: When the cathode material is heated to 300 °C, the two peaks at around 65° become one peak, indicating the structural phase transition of the cathode material from the rhombohedral phase to the spinel phase. After heating to 400 °C, a new peak appears at about 30°, indicating the cathode material underwent another phase transition, from LiM2O4-type spinel phase to Co3O4-type spinel phase.

claims.claim: The separator has an endothermic shutdown peak starting at about 144 °C; after shutdown, shrinkage occurs, and at sufficiently high temperatures the separator collapses, causing internal short circuit.
claims.quote: The separator has endothermic peak starting at about 144 °C which is attributed to the shutdown temperature. At the shutdown temperature of separator during melting process, it is difficult for the lithium ion to transfer inside the battery... When the temperature increases too high, the collapse of the separator occurs, then short circuit occurs inside the battery.

claims.claim: The TTR is defined as the temperature at which the self-heat rate of the battery reaches 10 °C/min, and batteries with a higher TTR value are considered safer.
claims.quote: The TTR is defined as the temperature when self-heat rate of battery reaches 10 °C/min. TTR is the triggering temperature of battery TR. Batteries with a higher value of TTR will be more safe.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is triggered by the heat generation of negative material reaction when the battery is heated with 473.15 K.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway was triggered by the heat generation of negative material reaction when it was heated with 473.15 K

claims.claim: Heat dissipation at 293.15 K in the bottom part of the negative electrode material, applied before SEI membrane decomposition reaction starts, can effectively inhibit the occurrence of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Before SEI membrane decomposition reaction started, heat dissipation in the bottom part of negative electrode material at the cooling temperature was 293.15 K which could effectively inhibit the occurrence of thermal runaway

claims.claim: The suppression of thermal runaway by cooling at 293.15 K acts by suppressing the negative electrode material reaction, and when applied before SEI decomposition starts, the advance time has no effect on the suppression outcome.
claims.quote: The suppression of runaway battery heat was to suppress the negative electrode material reaction actually, and when the cooling temperature was 293.15 K, it could be conducted before the SEI membrane decomposition reaction started, which had nothing to do with the advance time.

claims.claim: The SEI membrane decomposition reaction is meta-stable and decomposes exothermically at 353.15 to 393.15 K.
claims.quote: The layer was meta-stable and could decompose exothermically at 353.15 ~ 393.15 K

claims.claim: The heat generated by the positive material reaction during thermal runaway was approximately three times that of the negative material reaction.
claims.quote: The heat generated by the reaction of the positive material was much higher than that of the other three reactions which was about three times of the reaction of the negative material.

claims.claim: Before the battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature, the gas release of LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries during stage B is 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol, and 0.058 mol, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas released from the LFP battery is 0.094 mol, the gas released from the LMO battery is 0.042 mol, and the gas released from the NCM battery is 0.058 mol.

claims.claim: The gas release which causes safety venting accounts for 29.1%, 75.2%, and 55.4% of the total gas volume in LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries, respectively, indicating that safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to electrolyte volatilization while LMO and NCM venting is mainly caused by gas release.
claims.quote: the gas release which causes the safety venting, accounts for 29.1, 75.2 and 55.4% of the total gas in LFP, LMO and NCM batteries, respectively. Therefore, the safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to the volatilization of the electrolyte. However, the safety venting of the LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by the gas release.

claims.claim: The NCM battery has the greatest total pressure change during thermal runaway at 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: The NCM battery has the greatest pressure change, P = 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.

claims.claim: During stage IV (uncontrollable redox reaction stage), the pressure change is about 2–9 times larger than during the first three stages combined, due to strong gas production and impact force generation.
claims.quote: The pressure change in stage IV is much larger than during the first three stages, which is about 2–9 times. This is due to the following two reasons: the battery produces more gas in this stage due to strong reaction, and the battery generates the impact force.

claims.claim: For LFP battery thermal runaway, gas release is the main cause of structural change and pressure increase, while for LMO and NCM batteries, impact force is the dominant cause of structural change and pressure increase.
claims.quote: For the LFP battery, gas release is the main source, and for LMO and NCM batteries, the impact force is the dominant cause.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition triggers other exothermic reactions that ultimately lead to thermal runaway, making the temperature at which SEI decomposition begins a critical threshold.
claims.quote: Identifying the temperature at which SEI decomposition begins is crucial since it triggers other exothermic reactions, ultimately leading to TR

claims.claim: The rate of SEI decomposition reaction, and consequently the heat release, increases exponentially with temperature due to higher temperatures providing more energy to overcome the activation energy barrier.
claims.quote: the rate of the SEI decomposition reaction, and consequently the heat release, increases exponentially with temperature. This behavior is due to the fact that higher temperatures provide more energy to overcome the activation energy barrier for the decomposition reaction.

claims.claim: The multiphysics model halts calculations if the maximum battery temperature exceeds 500 K, because exceeding this threshold can lead to a sudden temperature rise and potential explosion.
claims.quote: The model incorporates a user-defined condition, whereby the calculations are halted if the maximum temperature of the battery exceeds 500 K. This condition is crucial since exceeding this threshold can lead to a sudden temperature rise and potential explosion.

claims.claim: At 1C charge/discharge rate, the generated heat is insufficient to trigger SEI decomposition or thermal runaway, whereas operating cells at 2C rate was required to observe these effects.
claims.quote: The generated heat is insufficient to trigger SEI decomposition or TR, aligning with the fact that higher C rates increase the risk of TR by generating more heat due to higher current flow. Therefore, cells were operated at a 2C rate.

claims.claim: SEI formation initially grows rapidly during charge cycles due to diffusion limitation, but as the layer thickens its resistance increases, slowing growth rate and transitioning the system from diffusion-limited to kinetics-limited regime.
claims.quote: the SEI layer grows rapidly during the charge cycles due to being limited by diffusion. Over time, as the layer becomes thicker, its resistance increases, which slows down the growth rate. Consequently, the system transitions from being diffusion-limited to being limited by kinetics.

claims.claim: SEI formation led to reduction of cyclable lithium and significant capacity fade, causing battery B2 to reach cutoff voltage sooner than other cells under constant charge/discharge conditions.
claims.quote: the SEI formation led to the reduction of cyclable lithium, and significant capacity fade was observed. Due to this capacity fade the battery B2 reached the cutoff voltage sooner and remained in the rest stage until all the other batteries were discharged

claims.claim: At 70°C, Fe dissolution from LiFePO4 in 1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC electrolyte was 41,010 ppb, compared to only 116.6 ppb in 0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME electrolyte after 7 days storage.
claims.quote: The values are 116.6 ppb (0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME) and 41,010 ppb (1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC), which indicates that 0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME electrolyte can effectively impede the dissolution and improve the cathode stability at high temperature.

claims.claim: The self-heating outset temperature of the LiFePO4|Gr cell with 1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC electrolyte at 100% SOC was 57°C, while the cell with 0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME electrolyte showed a self-heating outset temperature of 86°C.
claims.quote: The self-heating outset temperature of LiFePO4|1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC + Separator|Gr cell is only 57°C (Fig. 7a). By contrast, the LiFePO4|0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME + Separator|Gr cell presents conspicously higher the self-heating outset temperature, which is up to 86°C (Fig. 7b)

claims.claim: After 200 cycles at 70°C, the capacity retention of the LiFePO4|Gr cell with 0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME electrolyte was 71.0%, significantly higher than the 54.8% retention of the cell with 1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC electrolyte.
claims.quote: After 200 cycles, the capacity retention of LiFePO4|0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME + Separator|Gr cell is up to 71.0%, which is significantly higher than that of LiFePO4|1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC + Separator|Gr cell (54.8%).

claims.claim: At 70°C, the SEI derived from 1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC electrolyte showed decreased inorganic Li2CO3 and LiF content and increased intermediate products (LixPOyFz and LixPFy), indicating the SEI formed at elevated temperature is not stable and continuous reaction between electrolyte and Gr anode occurs.
claims.quote: the heat-resistance inorganic Li2CO3 and LiF were obviously decreased, and large amounts of intermediate products (LixPOyFz and LixPFy) were existed in 1 M LiPF6-EC/DEC electrolyte-derived SEI at 70°C, indicating that the SEI formed at elevated temperature is not stable and the reaction between electrolyte and Gr anode occurs continuously.

claims.claim: When temperature was elevated to 70°C, the LiF content in the SEI derived from 0.8 M LiFSI-0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME electrolyte was significantly improved, attributed to high-temperature-promoted decomposition of FEC, LiDFOB, and LiFSI forming stable LiF.
claims.quote: When the temperature is elevated to 70°C, the content of LiF is significantly improved in 0.8 M LiFSI+0.2 M LiDFOB-3FEC/7TEGDME electrolyte-derived SEI, which is due to the high temperature is benefited to the decompsitions of FEC, LiDFOB and LiFSI, thus, lots of highly stable LiF can be formed.

claims.claim: T1 (onset temperature for self-heating) monotonically increases from 353 to 377 K as SOC decreases from 120% to 50% in NCM cells.
claims.quote: T1 monotonically increases from 353 to 377 K with SOC decreasing from 120% to 50%.

claims.claim: A decreased SOC results in an increase in cell thermal stability, thereby increasing T1 (onset temperature for self-heating).
claims.quote: A decreased SOC results in an increase in cell thermal stability, and thus increases T1.

claims.claim: T3 (maximum temperature during thermal runaway) decreases with decreased SOC, indicating the cell is less reactive with suppressed exothermic reactions at lower SOC.
claims.quote: T3 is decreased with decreased SOC, indicating that the corresponding cell is less reactive with suppressed exothermic reactions.

claims.claim: After T2 (separator collapsing temperature), pressure rapidly increases to its peak magnitude P3, and the variations in P3 between cells with different SOCs are much more obvious than those of T3, indicating that total released gas amount greatly differs for batteries with various SOCs.
claims.quote: the variations for P3 between cells with different SOCs are much more obvious than those of T3, indicating that the total amount of released gas greatly differs for batteries with various SOCs.

claims.claim: The activation energies for violent TR process reactions are generally lower than those of the self-heating process reactions, causing violent TR process reactions to proceed at a faster rate.
claims.quote: the activation energies for the violent TR process reactions are generally lower than those of the self-heating process reactions. This suggests that the energy barrier for these reactions is smaller, allowing reactant molecules to more easily collide with sufficient energy to overcome it. As a result, the violent TR process reactions proceed at a faster rate.

claims.claim: The kinetic parameters developed in the CRNN study are primarily applicable to 18650-type NCM111 cells, and incorporating more experimental data is essential to extend applicability to cells with different capacities and cathode materials.
claims.quote: the kinetic parameters developed in this study are primarily applicable to 18650-type NCM111 cells. To further enhance the applicability of the kinetic mechanism beyond 18650-type NCM111 cells, incorporating more experimental data into the CRNN training process is essential for capturing reaction kinetics across cells with different capacities and cathode materials.

claims.claim: LFP batteries exhibit the lowest overcharging tolerance but also the lowest TR hazard, showing only smoke release with no explosion or fire during overcharge-induced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: LFP batteries exhibit the lowest overcharging tolerance. However, LFP batteries exhibit the lowest TR hazard and only exhibit smoke release, no explosion or fire. NCM and LCO batteries exhibit more serious TR hazards.

claims.claim: Higher charging rates lead to earlier formation of large-scale short circuits, earlier TR occurrence, and increased risk of violent TR and gas explosions.
claims.quote: higher charging rates lead to earlier formation of large-scale short circuits, earlier TR occurrence, and increased risk of violent TR and gas explosions.

claims.claim: The peak expansion force (Fmax) at the end of stage III decreased with an increasing overcharge rate, likely because lower overcharge rates allow more time for thorough internal gas production side reactions.
claims.quote: the peak expansion force (Fmax) at the end of this stage decreased with an increasing overcharge rate, likely since lower overcharge rates allow more time for thorough internal gas production side reactions.

claims.claim: LCO batteries reached the highest maximum temperature of 917.2°C under high-rate (3C) overcharging, while LFP batteries demonstrated superior safety performance with only 252.7°C under 3C rate overcharge.
claims.quote: LCO batteries reached the highest Tmax (917.2℃) under high-rate overcharging, highlighting their high thermal hazard. On the other hand, the LFP batteries demonstrated superior safety performance with only 252.7℃ under 3C rate overcharge.

claims.claim: The TR risk increases in the order LFP > LCO > NCM, while the TR hazard increases in the order LCO > NCM > LFP.
claims.quote: The risk of TR increases in the order LFP>LCO>NCM, while the hazard of TR increases in the order LCO>NCM>LFP.

claims.claim: The onset temperature for thermal runaway in lithium-ion cells varied inversely with the degree of lithiation of the negative electrode, meaning a greater degree of lithiation resulted in a lower onset temperature.
claims.quote: They found the onset temperature for thermal runaway varied inversely with the degree of lithiation of the negative electrode (the greater the degree of lithiation the lower the onset temperature)

claims.claim: In a 130 °C oven test of a 100 Wh LiMn2O4/carbon cylindrical battery with shutdown separator, the battery can temperature stabilized near oven temperature but jumped to approximately 190 °C after about 80 minutes, at which point the battery vented but did not ignite.
claims.quote: In a 130 8C oven test, the battery can temperature seemed to stabilize near the oven temperature, but after about 80 min, the can temperature jumped to (cid:3)190 8C. At this point, the battery vented, but did not ignite.

claims.claim: In overcharge tests of 1.5 Ah prismatic lithium-ion cells, low charging rates below C/5 did not result in thermal runaway, while higher rates at C did result in thermal runaway.
claims.quote: In overcharge tests, they confirmed the finding of Tobishima and Yamaki [24] that low charging rates (<C/5) did not result in thermal runaway, while higher rates (C) did.

claims.claim: Above 100% SOC in lithium-ion cells, the onset temperature of chemical reactions for the negative electrode decreased from greater than 180 °C to approximately 80 °C, suggesting metallic lithium plays an important role in thermal runaway during overcharge.
claims.quote: Above 100% SOC, the onset temperature of chemical reactions for the negative electrode decreased from >180 to (cid:3)80 8C. This suggests that metallic lithium plays an important role in thermal runaway during overcharge.

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests, with 18,650-size LiCoO2 cells, the nail temperature exceeded 600 °C when forced slowly into the cell to a depth less than 4.5 mm, but with fast deep penetration to 7.5 mm the nail temperature did not exceed 140 °C.
claims.quote: Dahn [34], using a nail with an embedded thermocouple, found that, with 18,650-size LiCoO2 cells, the nail temperature exceeds 600 8C when forced slowly into the cell to a depth <4.5 mm. However, with a fast, deep nail penetration (7.5 mm), the nail temperature did not exceed 140 8C.

claims.claim: Li2CO3 on the surface of NMC cathode tends to decompose into CO2 at a relatively low voltage of 3.8 V via the reaction 2Li2CO3 → 4Li+ + 4e− + 2CO2 + 1O2, and this decomposition produces highly reactive singlet oxygen (1O2).
claims.quote: it tends to decompose into CO2 based on the decomposition reaction of 2Li2CO3→4Li+ + 4e− + 2CO2 + 1O2 at a relative low voltage of 3.8 V (Figure 2a). Moreover, they proved that Li2CO3 decomposition will produce highly reactive singlet oxygen (1O2), which is extremely destructive to the applied electrolyte.

claims.claim: Singlet oxygen formation from NMC and Li-rich layered cathodes (LNMO) tends to occur at approximately 80% state-of-charge, and when NMC cathodes are charged beyond 4.5 V (e.g., 4.7 V or higher), oxygen evolution occurs from the surface.
claims.quote: found that it tends to occur at ≈80% state-of-charge (Figure 3a)... When NMC cathodes are charged beyond 4.5 V, like 4.7 V or higher, oxygen evolution will happen from the surface of the applied materials, which can be attributed to the recombination result of singlet oxygen escaped from the NMC lattice.

claims.claim: The Ni4+ content after charging governs the thermal stability of NMC cathodes; Ni is the least stable element and experiences the fastest reduction from Ni4+ to Ni2+ during thermal decomposition, while Mn and Co improve and maintain thermal stability, respectively.
claims.quote: it's noticed that Ni is the least stable element and experiences the fastest reduction from Ni4+ to Ni2+ during the thermal decomposition, hence it can be concluded that the Ni4+ content after charge governs the thermal stability of NMC cathodes. In contrast, Mn and Co plays an important role in improving and maintaining the thermal stability of materials, respectively.

claims.claim: When battery working temperature increases higher than 80 °C, more exothermic chemical reactions follow up to further heat the battery, leading to an undesirable feedback cycle and safety issues.
claims.quote: When the working temperature increases higher than 80 °C, more exothermic chemical reactions will follow up to further heat up the battery, leading to an undesirable feedback cycle and safety issues.

claims.claim: The oxidation of the carbon coating layer on LiFePO4-based batteries occurs when the potential is higher than 4.75 V in water-free electrolyte, or at 4.5 V in electrolyte containing 4000 ppm water, and gas content in water-containing electrolyte is larger than in water-free electrolyte.
claims.quote: the oxidation of carbon coating layer happens when the potential is higher than 4.75 V in the water-free electrolyte, or 4.5 V in the electrolyte with 4000 ppm water, and the gas content in the water-contained electrolyte is larger than that in the water-free electrolyte.

claims.claim: The exothermic decomposition of SEI at about 100–150 °C can lead to thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: it has been reported that the exothermic decomposition of SEI at about 100—150 °C can lead to the thermal runaway of lithium ion batteries.

claims.claim: Chain-structured carbonates (EMC and DMC) produced more gases than cyclic-structured solvent (EC) when lithium compounds (Li2CO3 and LiOH-H2O) were immersed in electrolyte and kept at 85 °C, contrary to conventional expectation.
claims.quote: The results show the chain-structured carbonates, like Ethyl methyl carbonate(EMC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC), produced more gases than the cyclic-structured solvent (ethylene carbonate, EC), which is in contrast to the conventional expectation that the cyclic-structured solvent is less stable and would decompose through a ring-opening reaction.

claims.claim: In LTO-based batteries with water content of 20 ppm, the volume of gases generated is very low; however, at 4000 ppm and 4% H2O, water reduction on the LTO surface is enhanced and produces large amounts of H2 and OH−.
claims.quote: When the water content is 20 ppm, the volume of gases generated from the LTO is very low. However, with the condition of 4000 ppm and 4% H2O, the water reduction on the surface of LTO is enhanced and produces lots of H2 and OH−.

claims.claim: CO2 and H2 generation content in commercial cylindrical cells increased fast when the cutoff voltage is higher than 4.15 V or lower than 2.8 V, and C2H4 generation increased fast with C-rate variation from 1 to 4 C.
claims.quote: the CO2 and H2 generation content increased fast when the cutoff voltage is higher than 4.15 V or lower than 2.8 V. (Figure 12c) Moreover, C-rate dependency of gas evolution was noticed, like C2H4, which increased fast with the variation of C-rates from 1 to 4 C.

claims.claim: SO2 gas evolution occurs in β-Li3PS4-based solid-state cells but not in Li6PS5Cl-based cells, and is attributed to decomposition of the lithium thiophosphate SSE under chemical reaction with high-active oxygen generated from the Ni-rich cathode.
claims.quote: SO2 generation happens in β-Li3PS4 based cells but not in Li6PS5Cl based cells... The produced gases were attributed to the decomposition of lithium thiophosphate-based SSE (β-Li3PS4) under the chemical reaction with high-active oxygen generated from the cathode.

claims.claim: In nail penetration, the contact resistance is usually several orders of magnitude larger than the nail internal resistance, so the total short circuit resistance is dominated by the contact resistance.
claims.quote: In the nail penetration scenario, the contact resistance is usually several orders of magnitude larger than the nail internal resistance, so the total resistance is dominated by the contact resistance

claims.claim: Under inhomogeneous contact resistance distribution in nail penetration, the maximum heat production was always 300% higher than under the uniformity assumption.
claims.quote: the maximum heat production under the inhomogeneous assumption was always 300 % higher than that under the uniformity assumption

claims.claim: The initial SOC of the battery is positively correlated with its gas production during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the initial SOC of the battery is positively correlated with its gas production

claims.claim: The lower flammability limit (LFL) of battery vent gas decreases with increasing SOC because at low SOC thermal runaway is less violent and produces more CO2, requiring more fuel relative to O2 for ignition.
claims.quote: LFL decreased with the increase of SOC. Because at low SOC, thermal runaway is less violent and electrolyte can react adequately, which produces a large amount of CO2. In this case, more fuel relative to O2 is needed for the ignition, corresponding to a higher LFL, and vice versa

claims.claim: The safety valve of lithium-ion batteries opens once internal pressure reaches a critical value, with different safety valve structures having venting pressures ranging from 1 MPa to 3.448 MPa.
claims.quote: Different safety valve structures have different venting pressures, ranging from 1 MPa to 3.448Mpa
